January 13, 2020

Dear Ms. Mills:

Thank you for visiting our office today. We understand sometimes you leave the office and then think of non-urgent questions you wish you had asked. Well, we’re here for you. Feel free to use the attached questionnaire to ask those questions via MyNortonChart. A staff member or provider will be happy to answer any non-urgent questions you may have.

Your feedback is very important to us! You may be receiving a survey through e-mail and we hope you will take the time to complete it. We value what you have to say and use your feedback to improve our patient care services here at Norton Healthcare. Thank you!

If this is a medical emergency, please call 911 or go to the nearest Norton Healthcare emergency department.

We are privileged to be your choice for medical care. Wishing you good health.

Author: Taiwalkar, Janak Ramesh, MD Service: — Author Type: Physician Filed: 01/07/20 1750 Encounter Date: 1/7/2020 Creation Time: 01/07/20 1509 Status: Signed Editor: Talwalkar, Janak Ramesh, MD (Physician)

BHMG Orthopaedic Surgery Clinic Note

BAPTIST HEALTH

Subjective

Subjective

CC: Follow-up (8 week follow up Acute pain of left shoulder )

HPI

Amy Mills is a 36 y.o. female. Patient returns the office today for follow-up of her left shoulder pain. In the interim, she has seen a neurologist and been recommended to have ACDF surgery at C3-4. She is scheduled to have this done with Dr. Shields in Louisville. She has also seen Dr. Tibbs at UK initially who also recommended surgery.

ROS: Constiutional:Pt denies fever, chills, nausea, or vomiting. MSK:as above

Objective

Objective |

Past Medical History Past Medical History:

We reviewed nerve studies brought in with her from Louisville read by Dr. lyer that show a chronic cervical radiculopathy invoiving predominantly C5 on the left and incidental finding of carpal tunnel syndrome that is primarily sensory.

Assessment

Assessment:

1. Acute pain of left shoulder

2. Cervical radiculopathy

3. Central stenosis of spinal canal

4. Adhesive capsulitis of left shoulder

Plan:

1. Recommend over the counter anti-inflammatories for pain and/or swelling

2. Adhesive capsulitis left shoulder with chronic left shoulder pain--patient seems improved from her capsulitis overall. There is still significant weakness in the shoulder and a burning sensation over the axillary nerve distribution. Again, presentation like this for any type of organic shoulder issue would be highly atypical and unlikely. We have shared that with her today.

3. C5 radiculopathy in the face of C3-4 > C4-5 cervical neuroforaminal stenosis--recommended she address the discrepancy between the planned surgery according to the patient and the new finding of a C5 radiculopathy on her nerve studies with whichever neurosurgeon she chooses to go with. She will potentially need surgery to address both the C3-4 and C4-5 issues.

4. Follow-up with me when necessary.

Shields, Christopher B., MD at 1/20/2020 12:00 AM

Author: Shields, Christopher B., MD Service: — Author Type: Physician Filed: 1/23/2020 5:29 PM Date of Service: 1/20/2020 12:00 AM Status: Signed Editor: Shields, Christopher B., MD (Physician)

OPERATIVE/PROCEDURE NOTE

FACILITY: NORTON HOSPITAL

UNIT/AGE/GENDER: N.PERIOP I 36 F PATIENT NAME/DOB: MILLS, AMY JO [DOB-4120]

UNIT NUMBER: EP02256299

ACCOUNT NUMBER: 10080087152 DATE OF OPERATION (S) /PROCEDURE (S): [DOB-2520] SURGEON: Christopher B. Shields, MD

PROCEDURE PERFORMED: Anterior cervical discectomy and fusion at C3-4 with insertion of a 6 mm PEEK cage filled with VIA graft and bone chips.

PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: Cervical disc osteophyte complex at C3-4. POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: Cervical disc osteophyte complex at C3-4. ASSISTANT: Tina Woodson.

DESCRIPTION OF PROCEDURE: The patient was placed in the supine position with the neck slightly hyperextended. The anterior aspect of the neck was shaved,

prepped and draped in the usual fashion. An incision was outlined in the upper aspect of the cervical spine on the right side. A cross-table cervical

Generated on 9/24/21 1:16 PM Page 33




NORTON HOSPITAL Mills, Amy IN NORTON 200 E Chestnut Street MRN: EP02256299, DOB: [DOB-4120], Sex: F

Louisville KY 40202-1800 Acct #: 200459740 Adm: 1/20/2020, D/C: 1/21/2020

[DOB-2519] - Admission (Discharged) in NH 4K (continued)

Real people. Remarkable care.

OP Notes (continued

spine x-ray was taken with a lumbar puncture marker. Once the localizing x- ray was taken, the incision was opened and dissection carried deeply to the platysma. The platysma was sectioned longitudinally and separated from overlying and underlying tissue. The anterior aspect of the neck was approached with the trachea and esophagus reflected medially and the carotid sheath laterally. The correct level was identified by a cross-table cervical spine x-ray. The annulus of the disc was incised and removed, and cartilaginous endplate and soft disc tissue were removed from the disc space at C3-4. The disc space spreader was inserted and then allowed to move under the posterior aspect of the disc at C3-4 along with a bony spur projecting posteriorly from the lower aspect of the C3 vertebral body and the upper aspect of the C4 vertebral body. The dural tube was identified and wide foraminotomies were performed on both sides. That resulted in excellent decompression of the C4 nerve root. The surfaces of C3 and C4 were made parallel using a 6 mm rasp. A 6 mm PEEK cage filled with bone chips and VIA graft was inserted into the disc space at C3-4 and countersunk approximately 2 mm. The entire area was irrigated through copiously with Bacitracin solution and saline and hemostasis was secured. At that point, Dr. John Johnson of the orthopedic spine service entered the operating room to proceed with placement of cervical plate.

Tina Woodson was first assistant during this operation and she greatly facilitated this performance with control of blood loss and muscle retraction.

Christopher B. Shields, MD D: [DOB-1603] 10:23:33

T: [DOB-1604] 10:46:00/MODL Job I.D. 553279/868811688

OP Notes (continued

UNIT/AGE/GENDER: N.PERIOP I 36 F PATIENT NAME/DOB: MILLS, AMY JO [DOB-4120] UNIT NUMBER: EP02256299

ACCOUNT NUMBER: 10080087152

DATE OF OPERATION (S) /PROCEDURE(S): [DOB-1606]

SURGEON: John Johnson, MD

PROCEDURE PERFORMED: Anterior Anodyne cervical stabilizing plate, C3-C4. PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: Herniated nucleus pulposus, C3 to C4. POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: Herniated nucleus pulposus, C3 to C4.

FIRST ASSISTANT: Kristi Turner, APRN

Kristi was present during the instrumentation and closure of the wound. PROCEDURE: After the patient had undergone a C3-4 discectomy and bone grafting by Dr. Christopher Shields, which will be dictated as a separate

procedure, the procedure was then turned over to the orthopedic spine team.

Using a caliper, a 13 mm in length Anodyne cervical stabilizing plate was sized to fit across from C3 to C4 and was transfixed to the vertebral body

with 12 mm self-tapping screws, 2 into the vertebral body of C3 and 2 into the vertebral body of C4. All screws at the time of surgery had firm fixation by fill in bone. Lateral x-ray was taken to verify position of the plate, bone

graft, and screws. One screw appeared to be slightly lower on the lateral at C3 level; however, it still appeared to be above the marker of the cage at the superior portion of C3. Because of the fact that it had good fixation in bone by fill, 2 x-rays were taken. Still, the position seemed to be a little bit

inferior on the x-rays, but the cervical spine appeared to be somewhat oblique, and it was felt, because of the good fixation, that we would accept this. The wound was then closed in layers, sterile dressing was applied, and the patient was taken to the recovery room for further postoperative care.

John Johnson, MD

MEDICATION INSTRUCTIONS PER ANESTHESIA GUIDELINES: ONLY TAKE these STOP these medications 2 medications the morning of | weeks prior to surgery: surgery WITH SIP OF

WATER You may have a Norco pill if No Advil, Aleve, Ibuprofen, needed Motrin, diclofenac

CONTINUE ALL OTHER MEDICATIONS NOT LISTED ABOVE UNTIL THE NIGHT BEFORE SURGERY

*IF YOU ARE CURRENTLY ON A BLOOD THINNER, ANTIPLATELET, AND/OR ANTI-COAGULANT, YOU MUST CONTACT THE PRESCRIBING DOCTOR AND YOUR SURGEON FOR INSTRUCTIONS ON WHEN TO STOP THE MEDICATIONS PRIOR TO SURGERY*

*A PRESCRIPTION FOR MUPIROCIN/BACTROBAN WILL BE SENT TO YOUR PHARMACY AND WILL BE AVAILABLE IN A FEW DAYS. PLEASE PICK UP AND USE AS DIRECTED--IF YOU HAVE PROBLEMS OBTAINING THIS MEDICINE, PLEASE CALL US AT 502- 629-7709

INSTRUCTIONS FOR YOUR SURGERY:

“Bathe with Chlorhexide (CHG) as directed on your Bathing Instruction Sheet or as instructed by your Surgeon *Call your Doctor if any changes in your health occur such as fever, flu or sores on your skin

*Do not eat or drink anything after midnight--UNLESS OTHERWISE DIRECTED BY YOUR SURGEON *No makeup, perfume, cologne or contacts---REMOVE ALL JEWELRY AND BODY PIERCINGS*

*You may brush your teeth

*Arrive day and time as directed by your Doctor

*Leave your valuables at home

“Wear loose, comfortable clothing

*Leave luggage in car

*You must have someone to drive you home

“Bring your picture ID, insurance card, co-pay if needed and any papers your doctor may have told you to bring the day of surgery

Go to Norton Hospital at 200 East Chestnut Street. The Garage may be accessed from either Chestnut Street or Gray Street. The Hospital may be accessed from the Garage on the 2nd floor. Acknowledged: Parker, Marilyn, RN 01/21/20 1023 for Placing Order

Diagnoses

Herniation of cervical intervertebral disc with radiculopathy [M50.10]

Order comments: The following discharge instructions were discussed with the patient and they verbalized understanding When you go home you may get up and be active You may go up and down stairs, but make sure to hold on to the rail and have someone with you You can ride in a car, but cannot drive until you have been cleared by your surgeon You can take the dressing off of your incision on Friday and may begin showering on Saturday. Check your incision daily. Notify our office if you notice redness, swelling or drainage from your incision or if you have fever over 100.5 Keep your head elevated on 4-5 pillows for at least a week-DO NOT LAY FLAT!!! Stick with soft foods that are easy to swallow. Take small bites and chew your food well. Ice and popsicles help with sore throat You may not lift anything over 5 pounds until cleared by your surgeon and do not do any kind of work over your head Absolutely no nicotine products Absolutely no Advil, Aleve, Ibuprofen or any other kind of anti-inflammatory You cannot take Tylenol

her Orders (group 3 of 3) (continued

with your pain medicine. Your pain medicine has Tylenol in it. You cannot take both One of the side effects of the pain medication is constipation. You will probably need to take an over the counter laxative in addition to your stool softner. Please let our office know if you are having problems having a bowel movement One of the side effects of your muscle relaxer is drowsiness. If you begin to feel too drowsy that you cannot get up and safely ambulate, decrease the frequency of your muscle relaxer. Call our office at [PHONE-2195] if you have any problems. There is someone on-call 24 hours a day Follow up appointment with neurosurgery on 2/20/20 at 11:40 am with Stephanie Tichenor Nurse Practitioner.

Problem: Hemodynamic Status

Dates: Start: 01/21/20 Description: Disciplines: Interdisciplinary Goal: Patient's vitals signs are stable Dates: Start: 01/21/20 Expected End: 01/24/20 Description: Assess and monitor patient's heart rate, rhythm, respiratory rate, peripheral pulses, capillary refill,

color, body temperature, intake and output, labs and physical activity tolerance. Observe for signs of chest pain (note location, duration, severity, radiation and associated symptoms such as diaphoresis, nausea, indigestion). Monitor for signs and symptoms of heart failure (eg. shortness of breath, edema of feet/ankles/legs, rapid irregular heart rate, coughing, wheezing, white/pink blood tinged sputum, sudden weight gain, chest pain). Collaborate with interdisciplinary team and initiate plan and interventions as ordered.

Point: Antiemetics (Done) Description: Educate patient/family/caregiver on name, reason, dose, route, and frequency for taking the medication; special instructions

and precautions for preparing, self-administering, using, or disposing the medications in the hospital or home; any monitoring

that will be done to evaluate the medication and verify its effectiveness; significant side effects including drug-drug, drug-food interactions or therapeutic contraindications that may be encountered and how to avoid and respond to such factors; action to be taken in the event or a wrong or missed dose; next dose due. Point: Post-Op Pneumonia Prevention (Done)

Description: Explain to patient/family/caregiver that people are at risk for pneumonia when they lay around more than usual. Lack of

exercise and mobility also increases the risk for pneumonia. Instruct the patient to walk every 2 hours for short distances (if unable to walk, they should sit on bedside every 2 hours and

dangle legs unless contraindicated).

Learning Progress Summary

Patient Acceptance, Explanation, Handout, Verbalizes Understanding by MP at 1/21/2020

1045 Comment: Discharge instructions given

Topic: Disease Management - PostOp General Surgery (Done)

Point: Edema Management (Done)

Description: Educate patient/family/caregiver that patient may get puffy and look swollen. Need to reposition frequently, elevate

extremities above heart level if possible, use ice packs as ordered, maintain fluid and diet control.

Learning Progress Summary Patient Acceptance, Explanation, Verbalizes Understanding by FW at 1/20/2020 2230 Significant Other Acceptance, Explanation, Verbalizes Understanding by FW at 1/20/2020 2230

Point: Incision/Wound Care (Done)

Description: Educate patient/family/caregiver on how to care for incision/wound to include: how to clean, when to clean and any special instruction for cleaning.

Know how to care for your wound. Emphasize good hand hygiene before and after caring for the the wound.

Teach about signs and symptoms of infection and when to notify healthcare provider.

Teach to tell family and friends to never touch wound or bandage and to clean their hands before and after visiting.

Instruct to keep incision/wound as clean and dry as possible.

Caution against: scrubbing or rubbing the incision, removing the tape strips, using lotion or powder around the incision,

exposing the incision to sunlight, taking a bath unless the incision can be kept dry.

Learning Progress Summary Patient Acceptance, Explanation, Verbalizes Understanding by FW at 1/20/2020 2230 Significant Other Acceptance, Explanation, Verbalizes Understanding by FW at 1/20/2020 2230

Point: Bandage/Dressing Care (Done)

Description: Educate patient/family/caregiver on how to care for bandage/dressing to include: how to change, when to change and any special instruction for changing. Demonstrate changing a bandage/dressing and receive a return demonstation. Teach to keep bandage/dressing clean and dry as possible. Caution against: using lots of tape, using lotion or powder around bandage, getting bandage/dressing wet when taking a bath or shower. Emphasize good hand hygiene. If patient is not to change bandage/dressing, instruct him/her to make follow-up appointment with healthcare provider to have bandage/dressing changed. Encourage the patient/family/caregiver to ask any questions about bandage/dressing care.

Learning Progress Summary Patient Acceptance, Explanation, Verbalizes Understanding by FW at 1/20/2020 2230 Significant Other Acceptance, Explanation, Verbalizes Understanding by FW at 1/20/2020 2230

Point: Incision Support (Done)

Description: Demonstrate to patient/family/caregiver how to support the incision while coughing and moving around.

Point: Explain Pain Rating Scale (Resolved)

Description: Educate patient/family/caregiver on the appropriate pain rating scale. Encourage patient/family/caregiver to monitor patient's pain.

Patient Friendly Description: -Your nurse will ask you to rate our pain on a scale of zero (0), for no pain, to ten (10), the worst pain you can experience.

-Your nurse will ask you to set a pain goal within zero (0) to ten (10) so that he or she can be sure that what we are doing is working.

-Mild pain is 1-3, Moderate pain is 4-6 and severe pain is 7-10. -We may not be able to take away ALL pain, so try to identify a point that pain would be manageable.

-So if your pain goal is 4 or less and you are rating your pain at 7, your nurse would give you a pain medication and then re- check your pain to ensure it is moving toward your pain goal of 4.

Learning Progress Summary

Patient Acceptance, Explanation, Handout, Verbalizes Understanding, Teach Back by JD at 1/15/2020 1721

Topic: Pressure Ulcer Prevention (Done)

Point: Positioning/Pressure Relief (Done) Description: Educate patient/family/caregiver on positioning and pressure relief. When in bed, teach that it is important to reposition at least every 2 hours. When in a chair, reposition at least every hour, if they are able to shift themselves, they should do so every 15 minutes. Encourage patient to sit up in chair for all meals unless contraindicated.

Patient Acceptance, Explanation, Verbalizes Understanding by FW at 1/20/2020 2230 Significant Other Acceptance, Explanation, Verbalizes Understanding by FW at 1/20/2020 2230

Point: Skin care (Done)

Description: Educate patient/family/caregiver that skin should be inspected at least once daily. Use creams or oils on skin to maintain adequate skin moisture content. Avoid cold or dry air. Avoid temperature extremes. Prevent skin breakdown that could lead to bed sores. Have heels float in the air over edge of pillow.

Point: Pain Medication Actions & Side Effects (Done)

Description: Educate patient/family/caregiver that pain medication can cause constipation. Explain that a stool softener might need to be given while taking pain medication.

Educate patient/family/caregiver to take pain medication with food to prevent nausea.

Provide medication specific handouts when available.

Patient Friendly Description:

-IV Pain Medication Your nurse can call your doctor for an order and administer IV pain medication if indicated. This can be given through your IV and lasts 1-2 hours. This may make you feel sleepy or dizzy.

Description:

Explain to patient/family/caregiver that there are other ways of controlling pain than medication. The following are suggestions: position change, aromatherapy, deep slow breathing, distraction, quiet environment, imagery, heat therapy and/or cold therapy, laughter, massage, music, physical therapy, and touch therapy.

Topic: Infection Prevention (In Progress)

Point: Multi-drug Resistant Organism (Done) Description: Educate patient/family/caregiver about healthcare associate infection prevention strategies. Teach hand hygiene and wearing of gloves, gowns and mask when appropriate.

Explain that an MDRO is a strain of bacteria that is resistant to common antibiotics used to treat infections. Infections can vary, depending on the organism. MDROs can cause skin infections (boils, abscesses), urinary tract infections, blood stream infections, and pneumonia, and they can infect wounds, the respiratory tract and surgical sites.

Learning Progress Summary Patient Acceptance, Explanation, Verbalizes Understanding by FW at 1/20/2020 2230 Significant Other Acceptance, Explanation, Verbalizes Understanding by FW at 1/20/2020 2230

Point: Protect Others from Infection (Done)

Description:

Educate on respiratory hygiene and cough etiquette. To protect from further infection and to protect others from getting an infection, patient should cover nose and mouth with tissues when coughing. Encourage patient to place used tissues in a plastic bag that will not allow secretions to soak through. Patient should always wash their hands after using or handling used tissues.

Learning Progress Summary Patient Acceptance, Explanation, Verbalizes Understanding by FW at 1/20/2020 2230 Significant Other Acceptance, Explanation, Verbalizes Understanding by FW at 1/20/2020 2230

Point: Protect Yourself from Further Infection (Done)

Description: Educate that because patient is in a weakened condition he/she needs to avoid others who are sick as to not acquire another illness; should wear gloves, gown and face shield/eye protection.

Educate patient/family/caregiver on pneumovax vaccine. Point: Blood Transfusion (Done)

Description: Educate patient/family/caregiver on Blood Transfusions and transfusion reactions.

In a blood transfusion, donated blood is added to patient's own blood. A blood transfusion may also be done to supplement various components of the patient's blood with donated blood products.

During a typical blood transfusion, certain parts of blood are delivered through an intravenous (IV) line that's placed in the patient. A blood transfusion usually takes two to four hours, though in an emergency it can be done much faster.

A blood transfusion boosts blood levels that are low, either because the body isn't making enough or because blood has been lost during surgery, injury or disease

Tell me two important safety reasons why you need to ask for help when needing to go to the bathroom? 1. Instructions

Other instructions

Discharge instructions (specify) The following discharge instructions were discussed with the patient and they verbalized understanding When you go home you may get up and be active You may go up and down stairs, but make sure to hold on to the rail and have someone with you You can ride in a car, but cannot drive until you have been cleared by your surgeon You can take the dressing off of your incision on Friday and may begin showering on Saturday. Check your incision daily. Notify our office if you notice redness, swelling or drainage from your incision or if you have fever over 100.5 Keep your head elevated on 4-5 pillows for at least a week- DO NOT LAY FLAT!!! Stick with soft foods that are easy to swallow. Take small bites and chew your food well. Ice and popsicles help with sore throat You may not lift anything over 5 pounds until cleared by your surgeon and do not do any kind of work over your head Absolutely no nicotine products Absolutely no Advil, Aleve, Ibuprofen or any other kind of anti-inflammatory You cannot take Tylenol with your pain medicine. Your pain medicine has Tylenol in it. You cannot take both One of the side effects of the pain medication is constipation. You will probably need to take an over the counter laxative in addition to your stool softner. Please let our office know if you are having problems having a bowel movement One of the side effects of your muscle relaxer is drowsiness. If you begin to feel too drowsy that you cannot get up and safely ambulate, decrease the frequency of your muscle relaxer. Call our office at [PHONE-2053] if you have any problems. There is someone on-call 24 hours a day Follow up appointment with neurosurgery on 2/20/20 at 11:40 am with Stephanie Tichenor Nurse Practitioner.

Medication Information

Preparation for Your Discharge: Your providers may have already given you your prescription for pain medication prior to your discharge date. If this is the case, it is best to be prepared and have your medication filled and ready for your return home from the hospital. You may want to have your pain pills with you on your ride home if you are traveling a long distance. While at Home: + Use medication only as directed. * Remember medication takes time to work. Most oral pain relievers need at least 20 minutes to take effect.

Real people. Remarkable care.

Medication Information (continued)

+ Remember to take your pain medication before activity and at bedtime. Your provider may advise you to take your pain medication at regular intervals (such as every 4 to 6 hours).

Be sure to get enough rest. If you are having trouble sleeping, talk to your provider.

Use pillows to support you when you sleep and when you do your coughing and deep breathing exercises Try using the alternative methods such as heating pads or cold therapy, walking, guided imagery tapes, listening to soft music, changing your position in bed, aromatherapy, and massage can help relieve your pain.

Safe medication storage and disposal Medications should be stored by patients in secure locations and excess pills should be disposed of properly, to minimize the risk of diversion and inappropriate use. To find a prescription drug disposal location near you please

visit https://odcp.ky.gov

Third Party Apps and Epic

Patients of providers that use Epic software are able to configure 3rd party apps to retrieve parts of their health record for their own personal use, including lab results, allergies, medications, and immunization history.

For instructions, visit: https://open.epic.com/Tutorial/PatientAuthentication.

Norton Care Call

If you are going home after leaving the hospital you will receive a Norton Care Call

* Within 48 hours of discharge, you should receive an automated phone call from [PHONE-2062] (Caller ID will read Norton Care Call)

* You will be asked a few questions about your health and progress. Please answer the questions using the phone keypad.

* Based on your answers, a Norton Healthcare team member may call you to offer help and other instructions.

Notify our office if you notice redness, swelling or drainage from your incision or if you have fever over 100.5 Keep your head elevated on 4-5 pillows for at least a week- DO NOT LAY FLAT!!! Stick with soft foods that are easy to swallow. Take small bites and chew your food well. Ice and popsicles help with sore throat You may not lift anything over 5 pounds until cleared by your surgeon and do not do any kind of work over your head Absolutely no nicotine products Absolutely no Advil, Aleve, Ibuprofen or any other kind of anti-inflammatory You cannot take Tylenol with your pain medicine. Your pain medicine has Tylenol in it. You cannot take both One of the side effects of the pain medication is constipation. You will probably need to take an over the counter laxative in addition to your stool softner. Please let our office know if you are having problems having a bowel movement One of the side effects of your muscle relaxer is drowsiness. If you begin to feel too drowsy that you cannot get up and safely ambulate, decrease the frequency of your muscle relaxer. Call our office at [PHONE-2066] if you have any problems. There is someone on-call 24 hours a day Follow up appointment with neurosurgery on 2/20/20 at 11:40 am with Stephanie Tichenor Nurse Practitioner.

Medication Information

Preparation for Your Discharge: Your providers may have already given you your prescription for pain medication prior to your discharge date. If this is the case, it is best to be prepared and have your medication filled and ready for your return home from the hospital. You may want to have your pain pills with you on your ride home if you are traveling a long distance. While at Home: + Use medication only as directed. * Remember medication takes time to work. Most oral pain relievers need at least 20 minutes to take effect.

After Visit Summary (continued

Real people. Remarkable care.

Medication Information (continued)

+ Remember to take your pain medication before activity and at bedtime. Your provider may advise you to take your pain medication at regular intervals (such as every 4 to 6 hours).

Be sure to get enough rest. If you are having trouble sleeping, talk to your provider.

Use pillows to support you when you sleep and when you do your coughing and deep breathing exercises Try using the alternative methods such as heating pads or cold therapy, walking, guided imagery tapes, listening to soft music, changing your position in bed, aromatherapy, and massage can help relieve your pain.

Safe medication storage and disposal Medications should be stored by patients in secure locations and excess pills should be disposed of properly, to minimize the risk of diversion and inappropriate use. To find a prescription drug disposal location near you please

visit https://odcp.ky.gov

Third Party Apps and Epic

Patients of providers that use Epic software are able to configure 3rd party apps to retrieve parts of their health record for their own personal use, including lab results, allergies, medications, and immunization history.

For instructions, visit: https://open.epic.com/Tutorial/PatientAuthentication.

Norton Care Call

If you are going home after leaving the hospital you will receive a Norton Care Call

* Within 48 hours of discharge, you should receive an automated phone call from [PHONE-2075] (Caller ID will read Norton Care Call)

* You will be asked a few questions about your health and progress. Please answer the questions using the phone keypad.

* Based on your answers, a Norton Healthcare team member may call you to offer help and other instructions.

Hospital Course: Patient was admitted following elective C3-C4 ACDF by Dr. Shields and Dr. Johnson, which she tolerated the procedure well. she reported resolution of her preoperative neck and arm pain and felt her strength was improved postop. she had normal strength and sensation and neck was supple without hematoma. Patient was tolerating eating and ambulating without difficulty and was discharged home in stable condition.

Patient requested soft collar. She does not need collar post op for stability.

Discharge Exam:

Mental Status: The patient is awake, alert, oriented to person, place, and time. There is no dysarthria. Recent and remote memory appears normal for age. Fund of knowledge appears normal for age.

Cranial Nerves: Pupils were equal, round, and reactive to light bilaterally and consensually. EOMI without nystagmus. Visual fields were full to confrontation. Face was symmetric. There was intact light touch to V1, V2, V3. Hearing intact bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Shoulder shrug was normal. Tongue protruded midline.

Motor: Tone was normal in all four extremities without fasciculations, atrophy, or myoclonus. There were no abnormal movements. Strength was 5/5 in bilateral hand intrinsics, bicep, triceps, deltoid. Strength is 5/5 in bilateral iliopsoas, quadriceps, anterior tibialis, extensor hallucis longer, and gastrocnemius.

Reflexes: The reflexes are 2/4 for biceps, triceps, brachioradialis, patellar and achillies tendon bilaterally and symmetrically.

Sensory: Intact to light touch throughout all dermatones.

Cerebellar: There was no dysmetria. Fine motor coordination was intact.

Gait: Gait was normal based without ataxia. Tandem, heel, and toe gait were intact.

Incision: clean and dry no redness or swelling

Disposition: Home or Self Care

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Amy Mills is a 36 yr/o female who presents to the office today s/p ACDF C3-4 by Dr Shields and Dr Johnson on 1/20/20.

Symptoms have improved. Very pleased with symptoms improvement.

Left shoulder pain continues- has seen another orthopedic surgeon.

Swallowing getting better. Mild hoarseness intermittently.

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY Past Medical History: Diagnosis Date

* Anemia

during pregnancy

Anxiety

Depression

GERD (gastroesophageal reflux disease) Headache

Herniation of cervical intervertebral disc with radiculopathy Migraine

Wears glasses

PAST SURGICAL HISTORY

has a past surgical history that includes Tubal ligation (2015); Ectopic pregnancy surgery (2014); BREAST IMPLANTS (2016); Liposuction (2016); Abdominoplasty (2016); Cervical biopsy w/ loop electrode excision (2010); Bladder suspension (11/2018); Partial hysterectomy (11/2018); and Tonsillectomy and adenoidectomy (as a child).

EXAM

Constitutional: No apparent distress at time of exam.

Mental Status: The patient is awake, alert, oriented to person, place, and time. There is no dysarthria. Motor: Tone was normal in all four extremities without fasciculations, atrophy, or myoclonus. There were no abnormal movements. Strength was intact in bilateral upper and lower extremities.

Gait: Gait was normal based without ataxia.

Incision: clean, dry, intact. No signs of infection noted.

REVIEW OF DIAGNOSTIC DATA none

ASSESSMENT 1. S/P cervical spinal fusion

PLAN

Follow-up with orthospine as instructed. To see Kristi Warren APRN today. Follow all orthospine restrictions as discussed.

Use heat muscle spasms as needed for 20 minutes at a time.

No high impact exercise. Walk as much as possible.

Off pain meds.

Hoarseness will resolve within 12 weeks.

Return in about 3 months (around 5/20/2020).

Results Callback

If you had labs or diagnostic tests ordered and you do not receive a phone call, letter or MyChart message regarding your results within 2 weeks, please contact our office to receive your results.

Work Return/Restrictions No documentation.

Injection Instructions

If you received an injection, a small reaction is not uncommon. A low grade fever (up to 100.5°F) and some pain is also not uncommon during the first 24 hours, but unusual beyond. Call the office for a fever of 101.0°F or higher at any time. Any expanding redness or persistent drainage from your injection site should be brought to the attention of your doctor

After-Hours Instructions

For after-hours care, please call the office number and you will be routed to an on-call provider.

MEDICAL RECORDS

To request a copy of your medical records, email the Use and Disclosure Department through your MyNortonChart account by using the Ask Customer Service feature, or

complete a Norton Healthcare authorization form at https://medicalrecordrequest.nortonhealthcare.org

Once received, your request will be processed within 7 business days. Norton eCare Skip the waiting room for your non urgent health issue using Norton eCare through MyNortonChart.

Norton eCare providers are available 24 hours a day, seven days a week through two secure online options using your MyNortonChart account: a video visit or an eVisit.

For additional information on conditions treated and how to schedule an eCare appointment, visit NortonHealthcare.com/eCare.

Third Party Apps and Epic

Patients of providers that use Epic software are able to configure 3rd party apps to retrieve parts of their health record for their own personal use, including lab results, allergies, medications, and immunization history.

For instructions, visit: https://open.epic.com/Tutorial/PatientAuthentication.

February 20, 2020

Dear Ms. Mills:

Thank you for visiting our office today. We understand sometimes you leave the office and then think of non-urgent questions you wish you had asked. Well, we’re here for you. Feel free to use the attached questionnaire to ask those questions via MyNortonChart. A staff member or provider will be happy to answer any non-urgent questions you may have.

Your feedback is very important to us! You may be receiving a survey through e-mail and we hope you will take the time to complete it. We value what you have to say and use your feedback to improve our patient care services here at Norton Healthcare. Thank you!

If this is a medical emergency, please call 911 or go to the nearest Norton Healthcare emergency department.

We are privileged to be your choice for medical care. Wishing you good health.

Results Callback

If you had labs or diagnostic tests ordered and you do not receive a phone call, letter or MyChart message regarding your results within 2 weeks, please contact our office to receive your results.

Work Return/Restrictions No documentation.

Injection Instructions

If you received an injection, a small reaction is not uncommon. A low grade fever (up to 100.5°F) and some pain is also not uncommon during the first 24 hours, but unusual beyond. Call the office for a fever of 101.0°F or higher at any time. Any expanding redness or persistent drainage from your injection site should be brought to the attention of your doctor

After-Hours Instructions

For after-hours care, please call the office number and you will be routed to an on-call provider.

MEDICAL RECORDS

To request a copy of your medical records, email the Use and Disclosure Department through your MyNortonChart account by using the Ask Customer Service feature, or

complete a Norton Healthcare authorization form at https://medicalrecordrequest.nortonhealthcare.org

Once received, your request will be processed within 7 business days. Norton eCare Skip the waiting room for your non urgent health issue using Norton eCare through MyNortonChart.

Norton eCare providers are available 24 hours a day, seven days a week through two secure online options using your MyNortonChart account: a video visit or an eVisit.

For additional information on conditions treated and how to schedule an eCare appointment, visit NortonHealthcare.com/eCare.

Third Party Apps and Epic

Patients of providers that use Epic software are able to configure 3rd party apps to retrieve parts of their health record for their own personal use, including lab results, allergies, medications, and immunization history.

For instructions, visit: https://open.epic.com/Tutorial/PatientAuthentication.

February 20, 2020

Dear Ms. Mills:

Thank you for visiting our office today. We understand sometimes you leave the office and then think of non-urgent questions you wish you had asked. Well, we’re here for you. Feel free to use the attached questionnaire to ask those questions via MyNortonChart. A staff member or provider will be happy to answer any non-urgent questions you may have.

Your feedback is very important to us! You may be receiving a survey through e-mail and we hope you will take the time to complete it. We value what you have to say and use your feedback to improve our patient care services here at Norton Healthcare. Thank you!

If this is a medical emergency, please call 911 or go to the nearest Norton Healthcare emergency department.

We are privileged to be your choice for medical care. Wishing you good health.

From To Sent and Delivered Stephanie Tichenor, APRN Amy Mills 5/26/2020 2:15 PM Last Read in MyChart

8/7/2020 10:10 PM by Amy Mills

May 26, 2020

Dear Ms. Mills:

Thank you for visiting our office today. We understand sometimes you leave the office and then think of non-urgent questions you wish you had asked. Well, we’re here for you. Feel free to use the attached questionnaire to ask those questions via MyNortonChart. A staff member or provider will be happy to answer any non-urgent questions you may have.

Your feedback is very important to us! You may be receiving a survey through e-mail and we hope you will take the time to complete it. We value what you have to say and use your feedback to improve our patient care services here at Norton Healthcare. Thank you!

If this is a medical emergency, please call 911 or go to the nearest Norton Healthcare emergency department.

We are privileged to be your choice for medical care. Wishing you good health.

Clinical Notes Progress Notes Johnson, John, MD at 5/26/2020 1250

Author: Johnson, John, MD Service: — Author Type: Physician Filed: 5/26/2020 1:04 PM Encounter Date: 5/26/2020 Status: Signed Editor: Johnson, John, MD (Physician)

Subjective: Subjective Patient ID: Amy Mills is a 36 yr/o female.

Amy is a 36 yr/o old female who is seen today for follow up evaluation of neck pain. She is now 4 months s/p C3-4 anterior cervical discectomy and fusion with plate secondary to cervical stenosis. She states that she has been doing very well post-operatively. She notes that her pre-operative neck pain has completely resolved. She does complain of some right shoulder pain, but this is unrelated to her cervical surgery. She denies any pain, numbness, or weakness involving the upper extremities. She is satisfied with the overall surgical outcome.

The following portions of the patient's history were reviewed and updated as appropriate: allergies, current medications, past family history, past medical history, past social history, past surgical history and problem list.

Review of Systems

Constitutional: Negative for activity change, appetite change, fever and unexpected weight change. HENT: Negative for tinnitus, trouble swallowing and voice change.

Respiratory: Negative for shortness of breath and wheezing.

Cardiovascular: Negative for chest pain and palpitations.

Gastrointestinal: Negative for constipation, diarrhea, nausea and vomiting.

Genitourinary: Negative for difficulty urinating, dysuria, frequency and urgency.

Musculoskeletal: Positive for arthralgias. Negative for back pain, gait problem, myalgias, neck pain and neck stiffness. Skin: Negative for rash and wound.

Allergic/Immunologic: Negative for environmental allergies, food allergies and immunocompromised state. Neurological: Negative for dizziness, weakness, numbness and headaches.

Hematological: Negative for adenopathy. Does not bruise/bleed easily.

All other systems reviewed and are negative.

IN NORTON NORTON PHYSICIANS Mills, Amy

HEALTHCARE SERVICES MRN: EP02256299, DOB: [DOB-4120], Sex: F Res'people: Remarkable care 1930 Bishop Ln Visit date: 5/26/2020 peorle: STE 1200

Louisville KY 40218-1929 [DOB-2498] - Office Visit in Norton Spine Specialists (continued)

Clinical Notes (continued)

Objective: Objective Ht 5' 3" (160 cm) | Wt 78 kg (172 Ib) | BMI 30.47 kg/m?

Physical Exam

Constitutional: She is oriented to person, place, and time. She appears well-developed and well-nourished. No distress.

HENT:

Head: Normocephalic and atraumatic.

Eyes: Pupils are equal, round, and reactive to light.

Neck: Normal range of motion.

Pulmonary/Chest: Effort normal. No respiratory distress.

Musculoskeletal:

Comments: On physical examination, she is oriented x3 and is a very good historian. She is an independent ambulator with a normal gait. She has a fairly normal appearing cervical lordosis. She is neurologically intact in both upper extremities with respect sensation and motor. Skin integuments are normal and there are no palpable masses. Extraocular movements are full, facial expressions are symmetrical, respirations are not labored.

Neurological: She is alert and oriented to person, place, and time.

Skin: Skin is warm and dry. She is not diaphoretic.

Psychiatric: She has a normal mood and affect. Her behavior is normal. Vitals reviewed.

Assessment and Plan: Assessment Amy was seen today for neck pain.

Diagnoses and all orders for this visit: Cervical stenosis of spinal canal

S/P cervical spinal fusion

| have spent 25 minutes in consultation with the patient today. | have also spent time extensively reviewing imaging with the patient. Her x-ray showed the instrumentation at C3-4 to be in good position. Based on her symptoms and radiology, | believe that the patient is doing very well post-operatively. She does not report any significant neck pain at this time. In regards to her shoulder pain, | have advised the patient to be seen by a general orthopaedic surgeon for further evaluation. We have discussed the do's and don'ts and | have answered all of her questions. The patient will follow up in 3 months.

Return in about 3 months (around 8/26/2020).

Results Callback

If you had labs or diagnostic tests ordered and you do not receive a phone call, letter or MyChart message regarding your results within 2 weeks, please contact our office to receive your results.

Work Return/Restrictions No documentation.

Injection Instructions

If you received an injection, a small reaction is not uncommon. A low grade fever (up to 100.5°F) and some pain is also not uncommon during the first 24 hours, but unusual beyond. Call the office for a fever of 101.0°F or higher at any time. Any expanding redness or persistent drainage from your injection site should be brought to the attention of your doctor

After-Hours Instructions

For after-hours care, please call the office number and you will be routed to an on-call provider.

MEDICAL RECORDS

To request a copy of your medical records, email the Use and Disclosure Department through your MyNortonChart account by using the Ask Customer Service feature, or

complete a Norton Healthcare authorization form at https://medicalrecordrequest.nortonhealthcare.org

Once received, your request will be processed within 7 business days.

Norton eCare

Skip the waiting room for your non urgent health issue using Norton eCare through MyNortonChart.

Norton eCare providers are available 24 hours a day, seven days a week through two secure online options using your MyNortonChart account: a video visit or an eVisit.

For additional information on conditions treated and how to schedule an eCare appointment, visit NortonHealthcare.com/ eCare.

Third Party Apps and Epic

Patients of providers that use Epic software are able to configure 3rd party apps to retrieve parts of their health record for their own personal use, including lab results, allergies, medications, and immunization history.

For instructions, visit: https://open.epic.com/Tutorial/PatientAuthentication.

May 26, 2020

Dear Ms. Mills:

Thank you for visiting our office today. We understand sometimes you leave the office and then think of non-urgent questions you wish you had asked. Well, we’re here for you. Feel free to use the attached questionnaire to ask those questions via MyNortonChart. A staff member or provider will be happy to answer any non-urgent questions you may have.

Your feedback is very important to us! You may be receiving a survey through e-mail and we hope you will take the time to complete it. We value what you have to say and use your feedback to improve our patient care services here at Norton Healthcare. Thank you!

If this is a medical emergency, please call 911 or go to the nearest Norton Healthcare emergency department.

We are privileged to be your choice for medical care. Wishing you good health.

Progress Notes

Khalily, Cyna, MD at 6/18/2020 3:09 PM

Author: Khalily, Cyna, MD Service: — Author Type: Physician Filed: 6/18/2020 3:59 PM Encounter Date: 6/18/2020 Status: Signed Editor: Khalily, Cyna, MD (Physician)

Subjective:

Subjective Amy Mills is a 36 yr/o female new patient referred by Dr. Shields seen today for Shoulder Pain (new patient visit regarding left shoulder pain)

. Symptoms involve the left shoulder. Symptoms have been present for at least 2 years. Symptoms began after a slip and fall. During the same fall she also injured her cervical spine. Her cervical spine injury caused the left upper extremity symptoms. When she had an ACDF she said that most of her symptoms were immediately resolved. However she has had persistent left shoulder pain. Pain is diffuse but mostly superior. The pain is described as burning stabbing and severe. Symptoms are present most of the time but are exacerbated by activity.

Review of Systems Musculoskeletal: Positive for joint pain and neck pain. Negative for back pain, falls and myalgias. LEFT SHOULDER Neurological: Positive for weakness. Negative for dizziness, tingling, tremors, sensory change, speech change, focal weakness, seizures, loss of consciousness and headaches. Radiating pains

Objective:

Objective Physical Exam

Constitutional: well-developed and well-nourished

Appearance: in no acute distress

Gait: normal

Assistive devices: none

Lymphatics: no palpable axillary adenopathy and no palpable supraclavicular adenopathy Right upper extremity edema grade: no

Left upper extremity edema grade: no

Skin: no cellulitis and no erythema

X-rays taken in the office today

3 views left shoulder: AP, axillary, scapular Y

Endings: Normal study. No fracture no dislocation. Normal bone and soft tissue shadows. There is no significant degenerative change noted

Assessment/Impression:

Assessment

1. Chronic left shoulder pain XR Shoulder Comp Min 2 Vw LT MRI Arthrogram Shoulder LT FL Arthrogram Shoulder LT S&l

Plan:

Plan Patient was allowed to ask questions and stated their satisfaction.

Discussed diagnosis and treatment options with patient and spouse

She has seen a couple of other orthopedic surgeons and continues to have no answers or relief. She had an outside noncontrast MRI performed which was nondiagnostic

| have carefully explained to them that although most of the symptoms from her cervical spine injury are resolved it is possible that there is residual nerve damage.

Recommend MRI arthrogram for a more sensitive shoulder advanced imaging

She can call for results

Results Callback

If you had labs or diagnostic tests ordered and you do not receive a phone call, letter or MyChart message regarding your results within 2 weeks, please contact our office to receive your results.

Work Return/Restrictions No documentation.

Injection Instructions

If you received an injection, a small reaction is not uncommon. A low grade fever (up to 100.5°F) and some pain is also not uncommon during the first 24 hours, but unusual beyond. Call the office for a fever of 101.0°F or higher at any time. Any expanding redness or persistent drainage from your injection site should be brought to the attention of your doctor

After-Hours Instructions

For after-hours care, please call the office number and you will be routed to an on-call provider.

MEDICAL RECORDS

To request a copy of your medical records, email the Use and Disclosure Department through your MyNortonChart account by using the Ask Customer Service feature, or

complete a Norton Healthcare authorization form at https://medicalrecordrequest.nortonhealthcare.org

Once received, your request will be processed within 7 business days. Norton eCare Skip the waiting room for your non urgent health issue using Norton eCare through MyNortonChart.

Norton eCare providers are available 24 hours a day, seven days a week through two secure online options using your MyNortonChart account: a video visit or an eVisit.

For additional information on conditions treated and how to schedule an eCare appointment, visit NortonHealthcare.com/eCare.

Third Party Apps and Epic

Patients of providers that use Epic software are able to configure 3rd party apps to retrieve parts of their health record for their own personal use, including lab results, allergies, medications, and immunization history.

For instructions, visit: https://open.epic.com/Tutorial/PatientAuthentication.

June 18, 2020

Dear Ms. Mills:

Thank you for visiting our office today. We understand sometimes you leave the office and then think of non-urgent questions you wish you had asked. Well, we’re here for you. Feel free to use the attached questionnaire to ask those questions via MyNortonChart. A staff member or provider will be happy to answer any non-urgent questions you may have.

Your feedback is very important to us! You may be receiving a survey through e-mail and we hope you will take the time to complete it. We value what you have to say and use your feedback to improve our patient care services here at Norton Healthcare. Thank you!

If this is a medical emergency, please call 911 or go to the nearest Norton Healthcare emergency department.

We are privileged to be your choice for medical care. Wishing you good health.

ey

FL Contrast Injection CT/ MRI 0 D, a :

6/24/2020 11:30 AM [PHONE-2654]

BAPTIST HEALTH CORBI N XRAY [PHONE-2655] 1 TRILLIUM WAY CORBIN KY 40701-8727

[PHONE-2656]

Left shoulder pain

FL CONTRAST INJECTION CT/MRI-

CLINICAL INDICATION: Left shoulder pain; M25.512-Pain in left shoulder

COMPARISON: None available. Total fluoroscopy time 2.08 minutes.

FINDINGS: Preliminary images show no fracture or dislocation. Preservation of the joint space.

The procedure and possible complications were discussed with the patient. She stated that she understood and wished to continue.

Fluoroscopy was used to localize a site for entry. Site was prepped and draped in the usual sterile fashion. Topical anesthesia was provided with lidocaine. A 22-gauge needle was then inserted into the left glenohumeral joint. A mixture of bupivacaine, iodinated contrast, and gadolinium as well as sterile saline was injected into the joint space. Some of the injection was extraarticular.

No evidence of a tear of the supraspinatus tendon on the study.

Patient was asked to exercise her shoulder and subsequently was taken to MRI.

IMPRESSION: Technically successful left shoulder arthrogram. No tear seen on this portion of the exam. MRI is pending.

This report was finalized on 6/24/2020 11:42 AM by Dr. Harold Reedy, MD.

Mills, Amy (MR # [PHONE-2657]) Printed by Interface, Rad Results Enterprise In [EDIRADENT] at 6/24/20 1:02 PM

Mills, Amy DOB: [DOB-4120] Unofficial Copy of Medical Record ay Khalily, Cyna 210 © Gray St #604 Louisville KY 40202 BH COR MRI Imaging Result Mills, Amy [DOB-4120] Female MRI Shoulder Left Arthrogram [DOB-2675] 1:14 PM [PHONE-2676]

BH COR MRI [PHONE-2677] M25.512 1 TRILLIUM WAY CORBIN KY 40701-8727

[PHONE-2678]

MRI SHOULDER LEFT ARTHROGRAM

CLINICAL INDICATION: M25.512; M25.512-Pain in left shoulder; G89.29-Other chronic pain.

COMPARISON: [DOB-2679]

PROCEDURE: Multiple planar images of the left shoulder were acquired in a high field strength magnet. Several pulse sequences were used to acquire the study. |V gadolinium was not administered. However, the patient was injected intra-articular prior to the exam.

FINDINGS: A small portion of the injection was extra-articular, thus accounting for fluid in the adjacent musculature.

There is no evidence of a tear of the rotator cuff.

There is signal in the superior labrum, concerning for an anterior superior labral tear.

No adjacent soft tissue mass is seen.

No acute fracture.

IMPRESSION:

1. Partial extra-articular injection.

2. There is signal within the superior labrum anteriorly concerning for a small anterior labral tear.

3. No evidence of a rotator cuff tear.

This report was finalized on 6/24/2020 1:36 PM by Dr. Harold Reedy, MD.

This 36 year old female presents for MA Notes, HPI, Neck Pain and Left Shoulder Pain.

History of Present Illness:

1. MA Notes Pt presents today for a telephone visit for a f/u PHQ-2 Moderately severe depression UDS ordered

Pt is due for UDS, med agt, tdap

Audio visit only, no capability for Audio/Visual visit at this time. Verbal consent for treatment via telephone was obtained after informing the patient of the risks and benefits.

2. HPI Patient initiated call. Obtained verbal consent from the patient to proceed.

Follow up instructions given

| spent minutes on the telephone call with the patient

3. Neck Pain The severity of the problem is moderate. The problem has worsened. The frequency of pain is daily. Location of pain is bilateral posterior neck. The patient describes the pain as Aching and Throbbing. The event(s) surrounding the occurrence of the symptom do not include injury. Aggravating factors include flexion and turning head. Associated symptoms include joint pain, muscle spasm and tenderness.

4. Left Shoulder Pain It occurs constantly and is worsening. Location: left shoulder. The pain is aching and throbbing. Context: there is an injury. The pain is aggravated by movement. Associated symptoms include decreased mobility, difficulty initiating sleep and joint tenderness. Additional information: MRI showed labrum tear.

Clinical Notes Progress Notes

Khalily, Cyna, MD at 8/6/2020 2:47 PM

Author: Khalily, Cyna, MD Service: — Author Type: Physician Filed: 8/6/2020 2:51 PM Encounter Date: 8/6/2020 Status: Signed Editor: Khalily, Cyna, MD (Physician)

Subjective:

Subjective Amy Mills is a 36 yr/o female patientseen today for Results (Follow up cisit regarding MRI of left shoulder) . Shoulder symptoms are unchanged. Symptoms have been present for at least 2 years. Symptoms began after a slip and fall. During the same fall she also injured her cervical spine. Her cervical spine injury caused the left upper extremity symptoms. When she had an ACDF she said that most of her symptoms were immediately resolved. However she has had persistent left shoulder pain. Pain is diffuse but mostly superior. The pain is described as burning stabbing and severe. Symptoms are present most of the time but are exacerbated by activity. She also describes a feeling of the shoulder being "forward" or feeling "out of place"

Review of Systems Musculoskeletal: Positive for joint pain and neck pain. Negative for back pain, falls and myalgias. LEFT SHOULDER Neurological: Positive for weakness. Negative for dizziness, tingling, tremors, sensory change, speech change, focal weakness, seizures, loss of consciousness and headaches. Radiating pains

Objective:

Objective Physical Exam

Constitutional: well-developed and well-nourished

Appearance: in no acute distress

Gait: normal

Assistive devices: none

Lymphatics: no palpable axillary adenopathy and no palpable supraclavicular adenopathy

Left Shoulder Exam

Tenderness The patient is experiencing tenderness in the acromion.

Range of Motion

Active abduction: abnormal Passive abduction: normal

Muscle Strength The patient has normal left shoulder strength.

Tests Apprehension: positive Hawkins test: positive

Other

Erythema: absent Sensation: normal Pulse: present

Assessment/Impression: Assessment 1. Superior glenoid labrum lesion of left shoulder, subsequent encounter 2. Pre-operative laboratory examination COVID-19, Novel Coronavirus PAT (Pre- Admission Testing): Reason for Testing: Pre Admission Testing

Plan:

Plan Patient was allowed to ask questions and stated their satisfaction.

Discussed diagnosis and treatment options with patient and spouse

MRI is read as a possible anterior labrum defect. This is what | suspected. | have discussed with them that the radiologist interpretation is of a "small" tear but again this could be responsible for for her symptoms. Also possible that it is not

However it is been over a year and she continues to have significant pain and is willing to undergo shoulder arthroscopy. | told her that shoulder arthroscopy with or without labrum repair

The risks, benefits and alternatives of surgery were discussed with the patient including, but not limited to:

Having a shoulder arthroscopy may put you at risk of having an infection or bleeding. Other parts of the shoulder, such as nerves, blood vessels, or ligaments may be damaged. Your shoulder may become stiff, numb, or more painful. Medicines, including anesthesia, and devices used during surgery may cause an allergy. Sometimes, the shoulder problem may come back after being treated successfully. If left untreated, the pain and problems you have with your shoulder may get worse. You may have trouble going back to your usual activities, including sports. Call your caregiver if you are worried or have questions about your procedure, medicine, or care.

Results Callback

If you had labs or diagnostic tests ordered and you do not receive a phone call, letter or MyChart message regarding your results within 2 weeks, please contact our office to receive your results.

Work Return/Restrictions No documentation.

Injection Instructions

If you received an injection, a small reaction is not uncommon. A low grade fever (up to 100.5°F) and some pain is also not uncommon during the first 24 hours, but unusual beyond. Call the office for a fever of 101.0°F or higher at any time. Any expanding redness or persistent drainage from your injection site should be brought to the attention of your doctor

After-Hours Instructions

For after-hours care, please call the office number and you will be routed to an on-call provider.

MEDICAL RECORDS

To request a copy of your medical records, email the Use and Disclosure Department through your MyNortonChart account by using the Ask Customer Service feature, or

complete a Norton Healthcare authorization form at https://medicalrecordrequest.nortonhealthcare.org

Once received, your request will be processed within 7 business days.

Norton eCare

Skip the waiting room for your non urgent health issue using Norton eCare through MyNortonChart.

Norton eCare providers are available 24 hours a day, seven days a week through two secure online options using your MyNortonChart account: a video visit or an eVisit.

For additional information on conditions treated and how to schedule an eCare appointment, visit NortonHealthcare.com/eCare.

Third Party Apps and Epic

Patients of providers that use Epic software are able to configure 3rd party apps to retrieve parts of their health record for their own personal use, including lab results, allergies, medications, and immunization history.

For instructions, visit: https://open.epic.com/Tutorial/PatientAuthentication.

Dear Ms. Mills:

Thank you for visiting our office today. We understand sometimes you leave the office and then think of non-urgent questions you wish you had asked. Well, we're here for you. Feel free to use the attached questionnaire to ask those questions via MyNortonChart. A staff member or provider will be happy to answer any non-urgent questions you may have.

We are privileged to be your choice for medical care. Wishing you good health.

OPERATIVE/PROCEDURE REPORT

Facility: Norton Pavilion Hospital

Patient Identification:

Name: Amy Mills

Age: 36 yr/o

Sex: female

DOB: [DOB-4120]

Medical Record Number: EP02256299 Account Number: 10091617656

Date of Operation/Procedure: 8/10/2020 Pre-Operative Diagnosis: left shoulder SLAP Post-Operative Diagnosis: Same

Procedure Performed: left shoulder arthroscopic #1 anterior labrum repair #2 acromioplasty

Surgeon: Cyna Khalily, MD

Anesthesia: General with supplemental regional Preoperative Antibiotics: Kefzol Complications: None

Drains: none

Specimens: None

Estimated blood loss: 5 mL

Description of Procedure: Patient was met in the preoperative area the operative extremity was identified and marked. They were then brought to the

Generated on 9/24/21 1:17PM Page 286




NORTON HOSPITAL Mills, Amy IN NORTON 200 E Chestnut Street MRN: EP02256299, DOB: [DOB-4120], Sex: F

Real people. Remarkable care Louisville KY 40202-1800 Acct #: 203419394 , ; Adm: 8/10/2020, D/C: 8/10/2020

[DOB-2847] - Admission (Discharged) in NH PAV Periop Services (continued)

OP Notes (continued

operative suite. After the satisfactory induction of general anesthesia,the operative extremity was prepped and draped in a normal sterile manner. Surgical time-out was then called to confirm that the patient had been given appropriate preoperative antibiotics, that this was the correct patient, correct procedure, and correct operative extremity.

A posterior portal was established in the glenohumeral joint was entered. An anterior portal was established.

The underside of the rotator cuff is intact. The articular cartilage of the glenohumeral joint was intact. As predicted on the MRI there was a discrete anterior attachment of the labrum at the 9 o'clock position. The bicep tendon and anchor were intact. An arthroscopic Freer was used to dissect the area of the tear and then a rasp was used to abrade the anterior bony glenoid.

The labrum was then reapproximated using a single bioabsorbable suture anchor.

An accessory superior lateral portal was established and scope was placed in the subacromial space. A complete subacromial bursectomy was performed and then the shaver was used to remove the underlying acromial spur. The top side of the rotator cuff was intact

All fluid and soft tissue debris was drained from the joint. The portals were closed with nylon, and covered with a dry sterile dressing.

The patient tolerated the procedure well and was returned to the recovery room

in satisfactory condition

8/10/2020 1:35 PM Cyna Khalily, MD

Procedure Orders

1. Interscalene Nerve Block [414888775] ordered by Noble, Ryan Matthew, MD

Procedure: Interscalene Nerve Block

Reason for Block/Indications: at surgeon's request and post-op pain management The procedure and alternatives were discussed as well as all clinically relevant risks and benefits. All questions were

answered. The patient (and/or proxy) agreed to proceed, informed consent obtained

Sedation: IV sedation, See MAR for details

(NH 113-149 sec) (AUD,NWC,NBH, 123-172 sec) (Pediatric 89-169 sec)Laterality: Left Procedure timeout performed

Procedure site has been marked and confirmed by Anesthesia Provider

Patient position: Sitting Prep: Chlorhexidine.

Procedure Technique: Ultrasound guided. Ultrasound used to visualize needle placement in proximity to nerve being blocked.

Needle type: Stimuplex 10 G 20 cm

Start time: 8/10/2020 11:46 AM End time: 8/10/2020 11:47 AM Injection made incrementally with aspirations every 5 mL.

Additional Notes: Patient was identified, consent checked, and timeout performed. Patient was interviewed and risks and benefits of anesthesia and peripheral nerve block were discussed prior to procedure, and patient was in agreement. In addition to the description above, the procedure was performed as follows:

1) Under ultrasound guidance, a 20 gauge needle was inserted and placed in close proximity to the brachial plexus at the interscalene level.

2) Ultrasound was also used to visualize the spread of anesthetic in close proximity to the nerve being blocked. 3) The nerve appeared anatomically normal.

Interscalene Nerve Block

Ordering provider: Noble, Ryan Matthew, MD 08/10/20 1150 Order Filed by: Noble, Ryan Matthew, MD 08/10/20 1151

Narrative:

Noble, Ryan Matthew, MD = 8/10/2020 11:51 AM

Procedure: Interscalene Nerve Block

Reason for Block/Indications: at surgeon's request and post-op pain management

The procedure and alternatives were discussed as well as all clinically relevant risks and benefits. All questions were answered. The patient (and/or proxy) agreed to proceed, informed consent obtained

Sedation: IV sedation, See MAR for details

(NH 113-149 sec) (AUD,NWC,NBH, 123-172 sec) (Pediatric 89-169 sec)Laterality: Left

Procedure timeout performed

Procedure site has been marked and confirmed by Anesthesia Provider

Patient position: Sitting Prep: Chlorhexidine.

Procedure Technique: Ultrasound guided. Ultrasound used to visualize needle placement in proximity to nerve being blocked.

Needle type: Stimuplex 10 G 20 cm

Generated on 9/24/21 1:17 PM

Resulted: 08/10/20 1150, Result status: Final result status: Completed

NORTON HOSPITAL Mills, Amy IN NORTON 200 E Chestnut Street MRN: EP02256299, DOB: [DOB-4120], Sex: F

Anesthesia on 08/10/20 (continued

Real people. Remarkable care.

Start time: 8/10/2020 11:46 AM End time: 8/10/2020 11:47 AM Injection made incrementally with aspirations every 5 mL.

Additional Notes: Patient was identified, consent checked, and timeout performed. Patient was interviewed and risks and benefits of anesthesia and peripheral nerve block were discussed prior to procedure, and patient was in agreement. In addition to the description above, the procedure was performed as follows:

1) Under ultrasound guidance, a 20 gauge needle was inserted and placed in close proximity to the brachial plexus at the interscalene level.

2) Ultrasound was also used to visualize the spread of anesthetic in close proximity to the nerve being blocked.

3) The nerve appeared anatomically normal.

4) There were no apparent abnormal pathological findings.

5) A permanent ultrasound image was saved to the patient's record. There were no apparent complications, and patient was conversant

throughout procedure. Patient tolerated procedure well. Please see MAR for drug dosages and times.

Diagnoses

Status post arthroscopy of left shoulder [298.890]

Order comments: SHOULDER * Regular diet at home * Leave dressing on and clean for 3 days. * May shower when dressing removed if there is no drainage from the incisions. If there is drainage, keep incision clean and covered. * Use ice or polar pack on and off for the next 2 - 3 days, no more than 20 minutes ata time. * Continue to use sling AT ALL TIMES. You may CAREFULLY remove to shower ONLY. * If you have a nerve block, it will wear off sometime today or tonight. Make sure to start taking pain medicine BEFORE the pain starts. * Call off for shortness of breath and signs of infection. * If you have pictures from surgery, bring them to your follow-up appointment.

Indications Status post arthroscopy of left shoulder [298.890 (ICD-10-CM)]

Remove dressing (Active)

Title: PreOp/PostOp General Surgery () Topic: Medications - PostOp General Surgery (Resolved)

Point: Antiemetics (Resolved) Description: Educate patient/family/caregiver on name, reason, dose, route, and frequency for taking the medication; special instructions and precautions for preparing, self-administering, using, or disposing the medications in the hospital or home; any monitoring that will be done to evaluate the medication and verify its effectiveness; significant side effects including drug-drug, drug-food interactions or therapeutic contraindications that may be encountered and how to avoid and respond to such factors; action to be taken in the event or a wrong or missed dose; next dose due. Give medication specific teaching material.

Point: Post-Op Pneumonia Prevention (Resolved)

Description:

Explain to patient/family/caregiver that people are at risk for pneumonia when they lay around more than usual. Lack of exercise and mobility also increases the risk for pneumonia. Instruct the patient to walk every 2 hours for short distances (if unable to walk, they should sit on bedside every 2 hours and dangle legs unless contraindicated).

Topic: Disease Management - PostOp General Surgery (Resolved)

Point: Edema Management (Resolved) Description:

Educate patient/family/caregiver that patient may get puffy and look swollen. Need to reposition frequently, elevate extremities above heart level if possible, use ice packs as ordered, maintain fluid and diet control.

Learning Progress Summary Patient Acceptance, Explanation, Handout, Verbalizes Understanding, Teach Back by AM at 8/10/2020 1438 Acceptance, Explanation, Verbalizes Understanding by SD at 8/10/2020 0947 Significant Other Acceptance, Explanation, Handout, Verbalizes Understanding, Teach Back by AM at 8/10/2020 1438 Acceptance, Explanation, Verbalizes Understanding by SD at 8/10/2020 0947

Point: Special Care Needs Related to Specific Surgery (Specify in Comments or Add Teaching Point) (Resolved) Description:

Educate patient/family/caregiver on the specific care needs, reason, how to perform and expected length of time needed due to surgery.

Point: Incision/Wound Care (Resolved)

Description: Educate patient/family/caregiver on how to care for incision/wound to include: how to clean, when to clean and any special instruction for cleaning. Know how to care for your wound. Emphasize good hand hygiene before and after caring for the the wound. Teach about signs and symptoms of infection and when to notify healthcare provider. Teach to tell family and friends to never touch wound or bandage and to clean their hands before and after visiting. Instruct to keep incision/wound as clean and dry as possible. Caution against: scrubbing or rubbing the incision, removing the tape strips, using lotion or powder around the incision, exposing the incision to sunlight, taking a bath unless the incision can be kept dry.

Point: Bandage/Dressing Care (Resolved)

Description:

Educate patient/family/caregiver on how to care for bandage/dressing to include: how to change, when to change and any special instruction for changing. Demonstrate changing a bandage/dressing and receive a return demonstation. Teach to keep bandage/dressing clean and dry as possible. Caution against: using lots of tape, using lotion or powder around bandage, getting bandage/dressing wet when taking a bath or shower. Emphasize good hand hygiene. If patient is not to change bandage/dressing, instruct him/her to make follow-up appointment with healthcare provider to have bandage/dressing changed.

Encourage the patient/family/caregiver to ask any questions about bandage/dressing care.

Description:

Educate patient/family/caregiver on discharge instructions. Review the physician's discharge instructions with patient and primary caregiver including follow-up appointments, diet, activity, medications and special precautions. If home care services

will be provided, review the role of the home care nurse and

when to expect the first visit.

Explain to family that they should prepare the home environment for safety/falls prevention if indicated (minimize trip hazards,

keep areas well lit, avoid slippery, wet floors, etc).

Use caution near pets (pets can be a tripping hazard).

If the patient has an incision instruct them to not allow pets to get near the incision so as to decrease the chance of infection.

Instructions

Activity instructions May shower when dressing removed

Diet instructions Advance to home diet as tolerated

Other instructions

Call doctor for: potential signs of infection Any changes to appearance of the site, including foul smelling drainage, increased redness, swelling, tenderness or odor

Call doctor for: difficulty breathing or shortness of breath

Provide home instruction sheet (specify) SHOULDER

* Regular diet at home * Leave dressing on and clean for 3 days. * May shower when dressing removed if there is no drainage from the incisions. If there

is drainage, keep incision clean and covered. * Use ice or polar pack on and off for the next 2 - 3 days, no more than 20 minutes at a

time. * Continue to use sling AT ALL TIMES. You may CAREFULLY remove to

shower ONLY. * If you have a nerve block, it will wear off sometime today or tonight. Make sure to start

taking pain medicine BEFORE the pain starts. * Call off for shortness of breath and signs of infection. * If you have pictures from surgery, bring them to your follow-up appointment.

Remove dressing

Medication Information

Preparation for Your Discharge: Your providers may have already given you your prescription for pain medication prior to your discharge date. If this is the case, it is best to be prepared and have your medication filled and ready for your return home from the hospital. You may want to have your pain pills with you on your ride home if you are traveling a long distance. While at Home: * Use medication only as directed. + Remember medication takes time to work. Most oral pain relievers need at least 20 minutes to take effect. * Remember to take your pain medication before activity and at bedtime. Your provider may advise you to take your pain medication at regular intervals (such as every 4 to 6 hours). * Be sure to get enough rest. If you are having trouble sleeping, talk to your provider. * Use pillows to support you when you sleep and when you do your coughing and deep breathing exercises Try using the alternative methods such as heating pads or cold therapy, walking, guided imagery tapes, listening to soft music, changing your position in bed, aromatherapy, and massage can help relieve your pain.

.

Safe medication storage and disposal Medications should be stored by patients in secure locations and excess pills should be disposed of properly, to minimize the risk of diversion and inappropriate use. To find a prescription drug disposal location near you please

visit https://odcp.ky.gov

Third Party Apps and Epic

Patients of providers that use Epic software are able to configure 3rd party apps to retrieve parts of their health record for their own personal use, including lab results, allergies, medications, and immunization history.

For instructions, visit: https://open.epic.com/Tutorial/PatientAuthentication.

Norton Care Call

If you are going home after leaving the hospital you will receive a Norton Care Call

* Within 48 hours of discharge, you should receive an automated phone call from [PHONE-2939] (Caller ID will read Norton Care Call)

* You will be asked a few questions about your health and progress. Please answer the questions using the phone keypad.

* Based on your answers, a Norton Healthcare team member may call you to offer help and other instructions.

Instructions PATIENT INSTRUCTIONS POST-ANESTHESIA

IMMEDIATELY FOLLOWING SURGERY: Do not drive, drink any alcohol, operate machinery, make important decisions, or sign any legal paperwork for the next twenty four hours after surgery or while taking narcotic pain medications or sedatives. You may experience a sore throat, mild nausea, dizziness, drowsiness, or a headache. These symptoms are normal after surgery and will subside in the next 24 hours. If you develop severe nausea and vomiting, a severe headache, or a high fever please notify your doctor immediately.

DO NOT TAKE TYLENOL WITH YOUR PERCOCET, DRINK PLENTY OF WATER, AND TAKE WITH FOOD.

CALL DR.KHALILY'S OFFICE IF YOU NOTICE ANY SIGNS OR SYMPTOMS OF INFECTION SUCH AS: INCREASED REDNESS, SWELLING, FOUL SMELLING DRAINAGE, FEVER, RED STREAKS, OR CHILLS.
