ANESTHESIA DISCLOSURE AND COnaezo co

nt oe “Khalil. Cyne (Provided in Connection with Surgical, Medical or Diagnostic 1 ek a ee services

TO THE PATIENT: Anesthesia is necessary to facilitate the recommended surgicin, meatcal or diagnostic procedure(s) you have consented to undergo and the use of invasive monitors (which are devices that enter the body to aid the physician in making decisions regarding a patient’s vital functions) may be necessary to assist with the provision of the anesthesia. You have the right, as a patient, to be informed about the provision of anesthesia and invasive monitors in connection with the surgical, medical or diagnostic procedure(s) to be used to diagnose or treat your condition.

\ { understand that, in connection with the surgical, medical, or diagnostic procedure(s) used to diagnose or treat ‘ry condition, the physicians and certified registered nurse anesthetists of Professional Anesthesia Services of Kentucky, PLLC, (my “Anesthesia Provider(s)”) and such associates, technical assistants and other health care providers as they may deem necessary, will be the providers of the anesthesia services and invasive monitors, if necessary.

\na physician who is not affiliated with Professional Anesthesia Services of Kentucky, PLLC (my “Treating Physician”), has already explained to me what my condition is, and | have already consented to the surgical, medical or diagnostic procedure(s) recommended by my Treating Physician to diagnose or treat my condition.

' | understand that my Treating Physician and/or Anesthesia Provider may discover other or different conditions which require additional or different procedures than those planned. | understand that the type of anesthesia service may have to be changed, possibly without explanation to me. | authorize the Anesthesia Providers to provide such other anesthesia services which are advisable in their professional judgment.

RISKS AND HAZARDS ASSOCIATED WITH ANESTHESIA:

| understand that all forms of anesthesia involve some risks and no guarantees or promises can be made concerning the results of my procedure or treatment. Just as there may be risks and hazards in continuing my present condition without treatment, there are also risks and hazards related to the performance of the surgical, medical or diagnostic procedure(s), including the anesthesia planned for me. | realize that severe complications with anesthesia can occur, including the potential for infection, blood clots in veins and lungs, hemorrhage, drug reactions, respiratory problems, cardiac/cardiovascular/other major organ problems, loss of limb function or sensation, paralysis, stroke, brain damage and even death. | further understand that, in addition to the complications noted above, the following risks and hazards may occur in connection with the forms of anesthesia listed below:

GENERAL ANESTHESIA Description: Total unconscious state, possible placement of a tube into the windpipe Technique: Drug injected into the bloodstream, breathed into the lungs, or administered by other routes Risks: Permanent organ damage; memory dysfunction/memory loss; minor discomfort; mouth, jaw or throat pain; hoarseness; injury to mouth, teeth, lips, gums, vocal cords, or eyes; need for post-operative ventilation; awareness under

anesthesia; and brain damage.

0 EPIDURAL / SPINAL Description: Temporary decrease or loss of feeling and/or movement to lower part of body Technique: Drug injected through a needle/catheter placed either directly into the spinal canal! or immediately outside the

spinal canal Risks: Headache; chronic pain; persistent back pain; bleeding/epidural hematoma; infection; nerve injury or damage; “total spinal”; potential to convert to a general anesthetic if the block fails or the procedure outlasts the block; and/or brain damage.

co MONITORED ANESTHESIA CARE WITH SEDATION (MAC or Conscious Sedation)

Description: Reduced anxiety and pain, partial or total amnesia Technique: Drug injected into the bloodstream, breathed into the lungs, or administered by other routes producing a semi- conscious state. This may include the administration of drugs to help alleviate anxiety, either alone or as a supplement to a

local or regional technique. Risks: Permanent organ damage; brain damage; memory dysfunction/memory loss; potential to convert to generalanesthetic if the sedation is not adequate; an unconscious state; and/or difficulty with breathing. Please know that MAC is a vigilant

monitoring of a patient’s vital functions while undergoing therapeutic or diagnostic procedures.

o/MAJOR / MINOR REGIONAL NERVE BLOCK ANESTHESH Right Left. Ieterscalepd Supraclavicular Infraclavicular Axillary Wrist Digital

Femoral Saphenous Popliteal Sciatic Ankle Eye Description: Temporary loss of feeling and/or movement of a limb Technique: Local anesthetic injected via a needle with nerve twitch monitor and/ or ultrasound guidance providing loss of sensation to the limb or area of the operation Risks: Infection; convulsions; weakness; persistent numbness; residual pain; injury to blood vessels (eye block-loss of vision); inability to breathe on your own which may require breathing assistance up to and including placement of a tube into the windpipe; collapsed lung; hematoma; temporary paralysis of the diaphragm; chronic pain; and medical necessity to convert to general anesthesia; nerve damage

RISKS AND HAZARDS ASSOCIATED WITH INVASIVE MONITORS:

Invasive monitors are devices that enter the body to aid the physician in making decisions regarding the patient's vital functions that cannot be obtained by non-invasive monitors. Certain risks involved in using invasive monitors include: injuries to blood vessels and/or the heart; infection; puncture of lung; collapse of the lung; injury to nerves; stroke; loss of digits; anxiety and/or discomfort. ,

ADDITIONAL RISKS TO SPECIFIC SURGERIES:

While complications are rare, different surgeries carry special risks. For example, spinal surgery has specific risks which you and your surgeon may have already discussed, such as the risk of blindness after surgery. Blindness can affect one or both eyes, may be partial or complete, temporary or rarely permanent. This risk is related to prone (belly down) position, as well as multiple other factors. Some risks are not necessarily dependent on the surgery or anesthesia alone. Your age, weight, and other health conditions may also affect your risk. Please do not hesitate to ask your anesthesiologist any questions you may have regarding specific risks to your surgery. Additional risks discussed:

GENERAL CONSENT:

She is about 3-week status post left SLAP repair. She said that the shoulder feels great. On exam the incisions are healed. She has full and unencumbered range of motion. She is not wearing her sling as recommended

Results Callback

If you had labs or diagnostic tests ordered and you do not receive a phone call, letter or MyChart message regarding your results within 2 weeks, please contact our office to receive your results.

Work Return/Restrictions No documentation.

Injection Instructions

If you received an injection, a small reaction is not uncommon. A low grade fever (up to 100.5°F) and some pain is also not uncommon during the first 24 hours, but unusual beyond. Call the office for a fever of 101.0°F or higher at any time. Any expanding redness or persistent drainage from your injection site should be brought to the attention of your doctor

After-Hours Instructions

For after-hours care, please call the office number and you will be routed to an on-call provider.

To request a copy of your medical records, email the Use and Disclosure Department through your MyNortonChart account by using the Ask Customer Service feature, or

complete a Norton Healthcare authorization form at https://medicalrecordrequest.nortonhealthcare.org

Norton eCare

Skip the waiting room for your non urgent health issue using Norton eCare through MyNortonChart.

Norton eCare providers are available 24 hours a day, seven days a week through two secure online options using your MyNortonChart account: a video visit or an eVisit.

For additional information on conditions treated and how to schedule an eCare appointment, visit NortonHealthcare.com/eCare.

Third Party Apps and Epic

Patients of providers that use Epic software are able to configure 3rd party apps to retrieve parts of their health record for their own personal use, including lab results, allergies, medications, and immunization history.

For instructions, visit: https://open.epic.com/Tutorial/PatientAuthentication.

September 10, 2020

Dear Ms. Mills:

Thank you for visiting our office today. We understand sometimes you leave the office and then think of non-urgent questions you wish you had asked. Well, we're here for you. Feel free to use the attached questionnaire to ask those questions via MyNortonChart. A staff member or provider will be happy to answer any non-urgent questions you may have.

Your feedback is very important to us! You may be receiving a survey through e-mail and we hope you will take the time to complete it. We value what you have to say and use your feedback to improve our patient care services here at Norton Healthcare. Thank you!

If this is a medical emergency, please call 911 or go to the nearest Norton Healthcare emergency department.

We are privileged to be your choice for medical care. Wishing you good health.

Clinical Notes Progress Notes Johnson, John, MD at 9/10/2020 1420

Author: Johnson, John, MD Service: — Author Type: Physician Filed: 9/10/2020 2:30 PM Encounter Date: 9/10/2020 Status: Signed Editor: Johnson, John, MD (Physician)

Subjective: Subjective Patient ID: Amy Mills is a 36 yr/o female.

Amy is a 36 yr/o old female who is seen today for follow up evaluation of neck pain. She has a history of an anterior cervical discectomy and fusion at C3-4 followed by placement of a cervical cage by me and Dr. Shields on 1/20/2020. She states that she was doing very well until she underwent shoulder surgery recently. She was seen by Dr. Khalily this morning and was advised to be seen in our office today. She describes an episode during her shoulder surgery where she jerked while going under anesthesia. She complains of neck pain and soreness. She has not been able to wear the sling for her shoulder due to the pain. She has not been seen by Dr. Shields recently. She denies any pain, numbness, or tingling involving her upper extremities.

The following portions of the patient's history were reviewed and updated as appropriate: allergies, current medications, past family history, past medical history, past social history, past surgical history and problem list.

Review of Systems

Constitutional: Negative for activity change, appetite change, fever and unexpected weight change.

HENT: Negative for tinnitus, trouble swallowing and voice change.

Respiratory: Negative for shortness of breath and wheezing.

Cardiovascular: Negative for chest pain and palpitations.

Gastrointestinal: Negative for constipation, diarrhea, nausea and vomiting.

Genitourinary: Negative for difficulty urinating, dysuria, frequency and urgency.

Musculoskeletal: Positive for neck pain. Negative for arthralgias, back pain, gait problem, myalgias and neck stiffness.

All other systems reviewed and are negative.

Objective: Objective Pulse 99 | Ht 5'3" (160 cm) | Wt 76.7 kg (169 Ib) | SpO2 100% | BMI 29.94 kg/m?

Physical Exam

Constitutional: She is oriented to person, place, and time. She appears well-developed and well-nourished. No distress.

HENT:

Head: Normocephalic and atraumatic.

Eyes: Pupils are equal, round, and reactive to light.

Neck: Normal range of motion.

Pulmonary/Chest: Effort normal. No respiratory distress.

Musculoskeletal:

Comments: On physical examination, she is oriented x3 and is a very good historian. She is an independent ambulator with a normal gait. She has a fairly normal appearing cervical lordosis. She is neurologically intact in both upper extremities with respect sensation and motor. Skin integuments are normal and there are no palpable masses. Extraocular movements are full, facial expressions are symmetrical, respirations are not labored.

Neurological: She is alert and oriented to person, place, and time.

Skin: Skin is warm and dry. She is not diaphoretic.

Psychiatric: She has a normal mood and affect. Her behavior is normal. Vitals reviewed.

Assessment and Plan: Assessment Amy was seen today for neck pain.

Diagnoses and all orders for this visit:

Cervical stenosis of spinal canal

| have spent 25 minutes in consultation with the patient today. | have also spent time extensively reviewing imaging with the patient. Her x-ray showed the instrumentation in good position, unchanged to her previous set of films. Based on her symptoms and radiology, | believe that the patient continues to do well post-operatively. We have discussed the soreness from her surgery and | have advised her to avoid any turning involving the cervical spine. If her pain persists, | have advised her to see Dr. Shields for further evaluation. We have discussed the do's and don'ts and | have answered all of her questions. The patient will follow up in 3 months.

Results Callback

If you had labs or diagnostic tests ordered and you do not receive a phone call, letter or MyChart message regarding your results within 2 weeks, please contact our office to receive your results.

Injection Instructions

If you received an injection, a small reaction is not uncommon. A low grade fever (up to 100.5°F) and some pain is also not uncommon during the first 24 hours, but unusual beyond. Call the office for a fever of 101.0°F or higher at any time. Any expanding redness or persistent drainage from your injection site should be brought to the attention of your doctor

After-Hours Instructions

For after-hours care, please call the office number and you will be routed to an on-call provider.

MEDICAL RECORDS

To request a copy of your medical records, email the Use and Disclosure Department through your MyNortonChart account by using the Ask Customer Service feature, or

complete a Norton Healthcare authorization form at https://medicalrecordrequest.nortonhealthcare.org

Once received, your request will be processed within 7 business days.

Norton eCare

Skip the waiting room for your non urgent health issue using Norton eCare through MyNortonChart.

Norton eCare providers are available 24 hours a day, seven days a week through two secure online options using your MyNortonChart account: a video visit or an eVisit.

For additional information on conditions treated and how to schedule an eCare appointment, visit NortonHealthcare.com/eCare.

Third Party Apps and Epic Patients of providers that use Epic software are able to configure 3rd party apps to retrieve parts of their health record for their own personal use, including lab results, allergies, medications, and immunization history.

For instructions, visit: https://open.epic.com/Tutorial/PatientAuthentication.

September 10, 2020

Dear Ms. Mills:

Thank you for visiting our office today. We understand sometimes you leave the office and then think of non-urgent questions you wish you had asked. Well, we're here for you. Feel free to use the attached questionnaire to ask those questions via MyNortonChart. A staff member or provider will be happy to answer any non-urgent questions you may have.

Your feedback is very important to us! You may be receiving a survey through e-mail and we hope you will take the time to complete it. We value what you have to say and use your feedback to improve our patient care services here at Norton Healthcare. Thank you!

If this is a medical emergency, please call 911 or go to the nearest Norton Healthcare emergency department.

We are privileged to be your choice for medical care. Wishing you good health.

' Reason for Appointment

1, Follow up; headaches

: History of Present HIness * General:

Ms. Amy Mills is a 36 y/o F who presents to visit for follow up.

. She has a history of chronic migraine,

More recently, she had left shoulder surgery last month, She was also noted on EMG in 12/2019 to have a chronic left cervical radiculopathy. She is most bothered by left posterior headaches, lasting hours, associated with nausea, blurred vision, and photophobia. She has had extensive workup for this in the past

‘ including brain MRI.

She takes Phenergan and Zofran prn for nausea, She takes

' Amitriptyline 10 mg qhs for headache prevention. She takes Maxalt ' prn for breakthrough headaches.

She has a lot of difficulty with fatigue and headaches.

' Vital Signs

WT 172.6, BP 126/84 mm Hg, Temp 96.6 F, HR 109 /min, O2 Sat % 98.

’ Examination

GENERAL APPEARANCE: Alert and oriented, no acute distress.

HEAD: Normocephalic, atraumatic,

EYES: Conjunctiva clear and lids normal, EOMI,

NECK/THYROID: Supple.

EXTREMITIES: without clubbing or cyanosis. No periph edema.

MUSCULOSKELETAL: no tenderness with palpation.

SKIN: no rashes, lesions, or ulcerations.

HIGHER FUNCTIONS alert, oriented x3, fluent, no dysarthria,

CRANIAL NERVES PERRL, EOM's intact, VFF, face symmetric, CN II - XT intact,

CEREBELLAR normal gait and station.

Messages (continued Last Read in MyChart [DOB-3298] 4:54 PM by Amy Mills October 7, 2020

Dear Ms. Mills:

Thank you for visiting our office today. We understand sometimes you leave the office and then think of non-urgent questions you wish you had asked. Well, we're here for you. Feel free to use the attached questionnaire to ask those questions via MyNortonChart. A staff member or provider will be happy to answer any non-urgent questions you may have.

Your feedback is very important to us! You may be receiving a survey through e-mail and we hope you will take the time to complete it. We value what you have to say and use your feedback to improve our patient care services here at Norton Healthcare. Thank you!

If this is a medical emergency, please call 911 or go to the nearest Norton Healthcare emergency department.

We are privileged to be your choice for medical care. Wishing you good health.

Cervicalgia (M54.2).

Medications reviewed. Take medications as prescribed. Side effects discussed and if experience any side effect stop medication and return to clinic. Counsel on medication compliance. Advised of proper body mechanics. No heavy lifting over 10 Ibs. No repetitive bending at waist. Change positions frequently, may apply heat/ice to affected area for 20 minutes for every 3-4 hours. Discussed benefits of maintaining healthy weight and , if no improvement return one week, will discuss ordering physical therapy if needed at next visit. If develop any signs/symptoms of cauda equina syndrome such as loss of bowel loss of bladder ; urinary retention; severe numbness/tingling in both legs call 911/go to Emergency room immediately. Patient verbalized understanding of plan.. Treatment plan discussed with patient. All questions addressed and answered. Instructed to follow up as needed if condition persist or worsens. 2. Assessment Other headache syndrome (G44.89).

Impression Headaches fairly controlled No changes to current medications Monitor and adjust as needed..

Patient Plan How to alleviate headaches -Apply ice. Hold a cold cloth or an ice pack to the painful area on your head or neck. Wrap the ice pack in a towel to protect your skin. -Relaxation exercises. Try relaxation exercises to lower stress. -Biofeedback. Biofeedback may improve your headaches by helping you to monitor how your body responds to stress. -Acupuncture. Acupuncture may improve your headaches and help you relax. -Take over-the-counter pain relievers. Your doctor may recommend you take nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs), such as naproxen (Aleve) or ibuprofen (Advil, Motrin IB, others). These medications may relieve your pain soon after your headache begins. -Take triptans. Your doctor may prescribe triptans, medications that block pain signals in your brain. Triptans often may relieve pain from your headache within two hours and help to control vomiting.

Assessment/Plan

Description

Acute laryngopharyngitis (JO6.0).

There's no cure for the common cold. Antibiotics are of no use against cold viruses and shouldn't be used unless there's a bacterial infection.

To make yourself as comfortable as possible when you have a cold, try:

Drinking plenty of fluids-Water, juice, clear broth or warm lemon water are good choices. Avoid caffeine and alcohol, which can dehydrate you.

Eating chicken soup-Chicken soup/warm fluids can be soothing and can loosen congestion.

Resting-If possible, stay home from work or school if you have a fever or a bad cough or are drowsy after taking medications.

Adjusting your room's temperature and humidity-Keep your room warm, but not overheated. If the air is dry, a cool-mist humidifier or vaporizer can moisten the air and help ease congestion and coughing. Keep the humidifier clean to prevent the growth of bacterial and molds.

Sooth throat-Saltwater gargle can temporarily relieve sore throat.

Saline Nasal Drops-can help relieve nasal congestion, even in children.

Zpack (#1) use as directed

Delsym (120ml) take 5-10ml every 12 hours as needed for cough

Medrol Dose Pack (#1) use as directed

follow-up visit if not improved. Reason for referral: if not improved.

INDEPENDENT MEDICAL EVALUATION

PATIENT NAME: Amy Mills

DATE OF BIRTH: [DOB-4119]

DATE OF IME: December 3, 2021

DATE OF INJURY: April 26, 2019

EXAMINING PHYSICIAN: Gregory B. Nazar, MD, FACS

CLINIC LOCATION: 9900 Corporate Campus Dr., #3000, Louisville, KY 40223 ATTORNEY: Aaron Whaley, Esq.

INTRODUCTION: The purpose of this report is to obtain a medical history, complete a physical examination, review medical record, derive a diagnosis with prognosis, and calculate an impairment rating if indicated.

The patient was self employed at the time of her injury, running an intradermal cosmetic business. Post-injury, she was no longer working in that capacity.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS: Amy Mills is a 39-year-old woman who fell on a wet floor while entering a bank on April 26, 2019. The patient states that she fell on her left side, injuring primarily her left shoulder. There may have been a brief loss of consciousness. She additionally injured her neck at the same time.

Prior to the injury, she had no previous left shoulder or neck related problems. She did have a history of headaches and blurred vision, as documented in appointments with her “eye” treatment providers. Further, one month before the date of the injury, the patient was seen in the emergency room with a severe headache. She was prescribed Norco 5 mg. She was diagnosed with possible occipital neuralgia and an acute headache. She had a normal range of motion of her cervical spine. A CT of her head was negative. Occipital neuralgia typically begins in the suboccipital region going forward into the head.

The patient’s injury occurred on April 26, 2019. She describes falling onto a hard floor that was covered with rainwater. She was seen in the emergency room the same day and diagnosed with a cervical sprain and a left shoulder contusion. She had a decreased range of motion of her cervical spine and left shoulder. A CT of the cervical spine showed no fractures and normal alignment with normal lordosis. She was treated with muscle relaxants and nonsteroidal anti- inflammatories.

9900 Corporate Campus Drive, #3000, Louisville, KY 40223 - [PHONE-3861] [EMAIL-3862] ills




Page 2 of 8

PATIENT NAME: Amy Mills

DATE OF BIRTH: [DOB-4119]

DATE OF IME: December 3, 2021

DATE OF INJURY: April 26, 2019

EXAMINING PHYSICIAN: Gregory B. Nazar, MD, FACS

CLINIC LOCATION: 9900 Corporate Campus Dr., #3000, Louisville, KY 40223 ATTORNEY: Aaron Whaley, Esq.

The patient then followed with her primary care nurse practitioner (Gross). She saw her on several occasions after the accident, where she was having left shoulder pain, headaches, and neck pain. An MRI of the brain was performed because of headaches on May 30, 2019, which was normal. Approximately a month-and-a-half afterwards, it was noted that she was having numbness and tingling in her left hand and arm with continued decreased range of motion of the shoulder and neck. She was treated, in addition to muscle relaxants and nonsteroidals, with a pain medication (Ultram). A referral to orthopedics was then made.

She was seen shortly after by a physician assistant, who recommended obtaining a cervical MRI to rule out radiculopathy, as she was having numbness and tingling going into her left arm with left shoulder and neck pain. At the time, she appeared to be having two separate problems, one involving her neck and the other the left shoulder.

The cervical MRI was performed on June 21, 2019 (approximately two months post-injury) and it showed a normal cervical lordosis. The only finding was at the C3-C4 level essentially, where she had a bone spur formation with a disc protrusion and mild to moderate foraminal stenosis on the right with no documented stenosis on the left. The other levels (C4-C5, C5-C6) showed some mild degenerative changes. The remaining levels were unremarkable. Thus, in summary, the MRI suggested possibly nerve root compromise on the right (the opposite side of her symptoms) possibly involving the exiting C4 nerve root, which extends into the medial neck and shoulder region. It typically does not go down into the arm. There was no spinal cord compression or frank disc herniation identified.

Because of the MRI findings, the physician assistant (Hale) for orthopedics referred her to neurosurgery to be reviewed. In addition, she followed with neurology for her headaches. An EEG was normal. It was suspicious that the headaches might be post-concussive in nature, although a concussion was not diagnosed in the emergency room visit after her injury.

The patient continued to follow with her primary care nurse practitioner, and over the next few months, she noted that her pain was becoming worse involving the neck and left shoulder, with paresthesias (numbness and tingling) going down into the left arm. She was evaluated by the physician assistant with neurosurgery on August 12 (approximately four months post-injury). They recommended referral to physical therapy. Also, their interpretation of the MRI was essentially the same, except that they felt that there may be a left foraminal stenosis at the C3-4 level. That would be different from what is in the MRI report.

Mills1343




Page 3 of 8

PATIENT NAME: Amy Mills

DATE OF BIRTH: [DOB-4119]

DATE OF IME: December 3, 2021 DATE OF INJURY: April 26, 2019 EXAMINING PHYSICIAN: Gregory B. Nazar, MD, FACS

CLINIC LOCATION: 9900 Corporate Campus Dr., #3000, Louisville, KY 40223 ATTORNEY: Aaron Whaley, Esq.

The physical therapy did not get approval and nor did she have any type of steroid injections into the neck. Thus, on her follow up with neurosurgery, it was their opinion that she “had not exhausted nonsurgical treatment,” and, therefore, no further treatment was recommended and to follow with the neurosurgery department at the University of Kentucky electively. No further treatment was offered to her at the time. There is no examination provided for her at that visit.

The patient followed also around the same time with her eye physician. She again was having complaints of blurred vision. Their eye examination was essentially identical to those performed prior to her injury.

The patient also continued to follow with orthopedics. The patient was seen on November 5, 2019. At that time, she had not received approval for physical therapy. She was still having left shoulder and cervical pain and described a burning pain going down her left arm into her fingers. The patient was referred to neurosurgery for a second opinion and saw Dr. Shields and his working partner, Dr. Johnson. At the time, she was having tenderness in and around the shoulder region and a decreased range of motion of her neck. A definitive source for her symptoms could not be readily identified. They did not feel that she had a rotator cuff tear but felt there was adhesive capsulitis involving the left shoulder. They also diagnosed her with a cervical radiculopathy based on her clinical symptoms.

Dr. Shields ordered an EMG nerve conduction study, which showed a mild, chronic C5 radiculopathy on the left. Based on the results of the EMG and her chronic neck pain, he recommended surgical treatment of the degenerative change at C3-C4 involving a discectomy and fusion. This was performed on January 21, 2020. The operative report is straight forward. There is no direct mention of an acute disc rupture or herniated fragment as being present.

Four months postoperatively, she had apparent resolution of her neck pain with no further pain going into the arm with numbness or tingling according to the records. She continued, though, to have left shoulder pain. She was then referred back to orthopedics and saw Dr. Khalily. He did an initial shoulder arthroscopy on the left for a SLAP tear of the anterior labrum of the shoulder. This surgical intervention did appear to be helpful, but the patient still is having pain in the side of the shoulder and feels that she might need a second operation with Dr. Khalily. The most recent MRI report suggests some tear of the posterior labrum as being present.

Overall, the patient describes that the cervical spine surgery made her feel better with respect to her neck pain in the short term, but in the long run, the pain remains present. She describes that there essentially has been no change in the numbness and tingling involving her arm and hand with the operative intervention.

Mills1344




Page 4 of 8

PATIENT NAME: Amy Mills

DATE OF BIRTH: [DOB-4119]

DATE OF IME: December 3, 2021 DATE OF INJURY: April 26, 2019 EXAMINING PHYSICIAN: Gregory B. Nazar, MD, FACS

CLINIC LOCATION: 9900 Corporate Campus Dr., #3000, Louisville, KY 40223 ATTORNEY: Aaron Whaley, Esq.

Her primary problems today are pain involving the neck, as well as the left shoulder, and with a description of numbness and tingling that goes into the ulnar aspect of her left arm that is intermittent. She holds her head to the right during the interview and history taking conversation. The patient has been continuing on medications, Maxalt and hydrocodone, for pain control.

She states that prior to the injury, she was very active and that now she has “trouble walking up a flight of stairs.” The patient denies having problems with her neck and shoulder prior to the injury. The patient is still under treatment for her left shoulder and states that she possibly might need a second arthroscopic operation for management of the problem. The patient has not been able to reestablish her home business of doing intradermal cosmetics, where she prints permanent cosmetic changes such as eyebrows, etc.

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:

OPERATIONS: Anterior cervical discectomy January 2020, left shoulder arthroscopic surgery August 2020, and vaginal hysterectomy.

HOSPITALIZATIONS: As above.

CHILDHOOD DISEASES: None.

ADULT ILLNESSES: None.

MEDICATIONS: Maxalt, hydrocodone, amitriptyline, Robaxin, Voltaren, and Zanaflex. ALLERGIES: NKA.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:

RESPIRATIONS: No shortness of breath, no dyspnea, no coughing, and no hemoptysis. CARDIOVASCULAR: No tingling, no palpitations, no temperature changes, and no chest pain. GI: No diarrhea, constipation, nausea, vomiting, or recent weight loss.

GU: No urgency, frequency, pyuria, hematuria, or nocturia.

MUSCULOSKELETAL: Left sided neck and left shoulder pain. NEUROLOGICAL REVIEW: Numbness and tingling involving the ulnar aspect of the hand.

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:

CONSTITUTIONAL: The patient stands 5 feet 5 inches and weighs 162 pounds. The patient is appropriate for her height and weight.

HEENT: Head: Normocephalic, no specific abnormalities present. Eyes: Full range extraocular movement, sclerae clear, conjunctivae clear, pupils equal and reactive to light. Ears: External auditory meatus clear. Nose: Nasal mucosa well hydrated. Neck: No lymphadenopathy, no venous distention, and no palpable thyroid.

CHEST/LUNGS: Clear, good air entry, no rhonchi, no rales, clear to auscultation and percussion. HEART: Normal sinus rhythm, no murmurs, and no bruits. BREASTS: Deferred.

ABDOMEN: Soft, nontender, no distention, no guarding, no hepatosplenomegaly, and bowel sounds are normal.

MUSCULOSKELETAL:

Cervical Spine: The patient demonstrates reduction in range of motion with left rotation primarily. Flexion remains normal. Extension is 25 degrees. She can rotate to 65 degrees on the right, 15 degrees on the left. Lateral bending is equal at 25 degrees. Thus, the primary findings on the cervical spine examination are the decreased rotation to the left. Spurling’s test is negative for cervical radiculopathy. She tends to hold her neck to the right during the interview but at other times can bring her neck into a neutral position relatively easily. The patient has some tenderness on palpation of the neck subjectively on the left. There is no muscle spasm or guarding that is identified.

Bilateral Shoulder Evaluation: The patient has tenderness on palpation of the shoulder. She can adduct to 85 degrees, abduct to 20 degrees, flex to 85 degrees, and extend to 20 degrees. Internal and external rotation appears to be normal.

NEUROLOGICAL EXAM: Her neurology is normal. She has a normal cranial nerve evaluation. Pupils are equal and symmetric. She has a normal accommodation reflex with constriction with convergence. There is no double vision. Extraocular movements are normal. Lower cranial nerve function is normal. She has a good shoulder shrug.

Her motor examination remains normal with all muscle groups. She has normal muscle tone. There is no muscular atrophy. Reflexes are equal and symmetric. There are no pathological reflexes that are present. Sensory examination is normal. The patient has a positive Tinel sign on the left elbow that would explain the intermittent numbness and tingling going into the ulnar aspect of the left hand. Spurling’s test is negative for cervical radiculopathy.

MEDICAL RECORDS REVIEWED:

1. Eye care examinations from 2014 and 2106, pre-injury.

2. Emergency room record from Baptist Health dated March 30, 2019, with diagnosis of headache and possible occipital neuralgia. CT head negative.

3. Baptist emergency room record dated April 26, 2019, immediate post-injury after the fall, with diagnosis of left shoulder contusion, right hand strain, and cervical strain.

MEDICAL RECORDS REVIEWED (cont.):

4. Records from Nurse Practitioner Gross (family physician) for left shoulder and neck pain and

6. Records from Physician Assistant Hale (orthopedics) dated June 12, June 21, and June 25, 2019.

8. Records from neurosurgery at the University of Kentucky dated August 12 and October 1, 2019, with diagnosis of left shoulder and neck pain, recommending nonsurgical conservative therapy.

9. Follow up eye evaluation by eye care dated August 20, 2019.

10. Orthopedic clinic records from Dr. Talwalkar dated November 5, 2019, with diagnosis of capsulitis left shoulder, cervical radiculopathy, and acute left shoulder pain.

11. EMG nerve conduction study by Dr. Iyer, demonstrating mild chronic cervical radiculopathy C5 on the left.

12. Records from Norton Hospital by Dr. Shields regarding cervical anterior discectomy with interbody fusion at C3-4, dated January 20, January 21, May 6, and September 10, 2020.

13. Record from Dr. Khalily dated August 10, 2020.

Nn

CLINICAL DIAGNOSES:

1. Cervical strain/sprain (chronic) secondary to fall on April 26, 2019, eventually treated with an anterior cervical discectomy with interbody fusion at C3-C4 with continuation of her neck pain and temporary improvement being noted post-surgery. The patient did not have preexisting neck pain according to the records provided.

2. Left shoulder pain with diagnosis of labral tear and possibly adhesive capsulitis, being followed currently by orthopedics and currently under treatment, possibly needing a second shoulder arthroscopic operative intervention.

3. History of blurred vision and headaches (chronic) with no change in her visual acuity.

Etiology unclear.

IMPAIRMENT RATING AND RATIONALE: The focus of the Independent Medical Evaluation today is on the cervical spine, as she appears to have two separate problems pertaining to the fall occurring on April 26, 2019. My expertise is in the field of neurosurgery and further, the patient is still undergoing orthopedic intervention and care regarding the left shoulder pain that she is having. Thus, she is not at MMI pertaining to her left shoulder issues.

Mills1347




Page 7 of 8

PATIENT NAME: Amy Mills

DATE OF BIRTH: [DOB-4119]

DATE OF IME: December 3, 2021

DATE OF INJURY: April 26, 2019

EXAMINING PHYSICIAN: Gregory B. Nazar, MD, FACS

CLINIC LOCATION: 9900 Corporate Campus Dr., #3000, Louisville, KY 40223 ATTORNEY: Aaron Whaley, Esq.

With respect to the injury to her cervical spine, the patient is at MMI and did reach such as of today’s visit on December 3, 2021, 11 months post-surgery for an anterior C3-C4 discectomy and fusion.

The patient’s primary complaints were that of neck pain with pain radiating into the left shoulder. It does not appear that she had an overall satisfactory response to the operative intervention but did have transient improvement. She still is having neck pain and holds her neck at times to the right.

Based on the American Medical Association’s Guides to the Evaluation of Permanent Impairment,

5th Edition, a patient undergoing a cervical fusion for management of a traumatic injury automatically qualifies for a DRE Category IV impairment (Guidelines Page 392, Table 15-5). Because of her residual pain that she is having, her impairment rating would be 27% whole person. The Guidelines state, “The individual may have a loss of motion or motion segment due to a developmental fusion or a successful or unsuccessful attempt at surgical arthrodesis.” This would clearly place the patient in the DRE Category IV based on the fact that she had operative intervention including a surgical fusion at the C3-C4 for management of her neck pain pertaining to the fall of April 26, 2019.

With respect to the neck problems, the patient would be fully capable of continuing to work in the capacity that she was doing prior to the cervical spine injury. Her restrictions would pertain to neck movements. Recommendation would be to move her neck as freely and as often as she would need to do. There would be no lifting, carrying, pushing, or pulling restrictions pertaining to the neck problems that she had or the type of surgery that was performed.

However, the patient clearly has problems related to the shoulder that will limit her capacity to push, pull, lift, or carry, as well as the ability to do her job that she was doing in her home business. Thus, since she is not at MMI with respect to her orthopedic problems, she would need to have her restrictions reevaluated after she has reached MMI with respect to her left shoulder injury and would need to be evaluated by orthopedics. Further, the patient will likely need a Functional Capacity Evaluation to more precisely evaluate what restrictions she has and has not pertaining to the left shoulder injury. As stated above, I do not believe that there would be significant restrictions pertaining to the operative intervention of her cervical spine.

CONCLUSIONS AND FURTHER TREATMENT RECOMMENDATIONS: To conclude, this patient had essentially two injuries. The first injury is to the cervical spine. The patient did not respond to medication therapy and/or time and eventually underwent surgical intervention for this with a C3-C4 discectomy and fusion, which at best produced modest results. The patient does not appear to have significant restrictions pertaining to the surgery or the injury to her neck, which is best defined as a chronic cervical strain/sprain.

Mills1348




Page 8 of 8

PATIENT NAME: Amy Mills

DATE OF BIRTH: [DOB-4119]

DATE OF IME: December 3, 2021

DATE OF INJURY: April 26, 2019

EXAMINING PHYSICIAN: Gregory B. Nazar, MD, FACS

CLINIC LOCATION: 9900 Corporate Campus Dr., #3000, Louisville, KY 40223 ATTORNEY: Aaron Whaley, Esq.

The primary problems that the patient is having pertain to the left shoulder with its restricted movements and possibly the need for further surgical intervention by her managing orthopedic surgeon, Dr. Khalily. She is clearly not at MMI pertaining to her shoulder, and a majority of her restrictions would pertain to what the end result would be pertaining to the left shoulder injury following appropriate treatment, be it nonsurgical or conservative. I would defer to orthopedic consultation for that assessment.

With respect to the cervical spine, she is at maximum medical improvement as noted above. The only restriction is to move her head as she needs to. She likely will need intermittent medication support in the form of a muscle relaxant and/or nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory. Other medication support would be related to her shoulder injury.

I do not feel that there are any further treatments needed for her neck (cervical spine) at this time. She does not need additional surgery, nor is she a candidate for steroid injections. It is doubtful that any type of physical treatment modalities (chiropractic or physical therapy) will be helpful. At best, she would need intermittent medication support as noted above in the form of nonsteroidals and muscle relaxants.

The recommendations at this point would be to go forward and complete her orthopedic treatment and then be seen by an orthopedic specialist for an impairment rating pertaining to her shoulder injury, as well as appropriate restrictions and determination of whether or not she would be able to go back to the work that she was doing. Likely, too, she would also need a formal functional capacity evaluation as part of the assessment.

Finally, the opinions that were expressed within this report are all within a reasonable degree of medical probability.

Sincerely,

Grégory B. Nazar, MD, FACS

December 29, 2021

Aaron Whaley, Esq. The Whaley Law Firm 712 Lyndon Lane Louisville, KY 40222

RE: PATIENT NAME: Amy Mills DATE OF BIRTH: [DOB-4119] DATE OF IME: December 3, 2021 DATE OF ADDENDUM: December 21, 2021 DATE OF INJURY: April 26, 2019

Dear Mr. Whaley:

Thank you for your additional question with respect to causation and the medical treatments that Ms. Amy Mills had undergone for her fall. As you know, she fell on April 26, 2019 and sustained neck and shoulder pain, resulting in the need for surgical intervention (anterior cervical discectomy at C3-4) on the basis that it was felt that this was responsible for her neck and shoulder pain. The patient did not have neck and/or shoulder pain prior to the fall. Thus, the cause of the neck and shoulder pain and the need for the anterior cervical discectomy appear to be directly related to her fall on April 26, 2019, as indicted under my clinical diagnosis number one, “Cervical strain/sprain secondary to fall on April 26, 2019, eventually treated with an anterior cervical discectomy with interbody fusion at C3-C4.”

Further, all of the medical therapies and treatments that she received pertaining to her neck and left shoulder (and possibly will receive in the future pertaining to her left shoulder), appear to be medically necessary and reasonable for the treatment of her injury.

Sincerely,

ar, MD, FACS

INFORMED CONSENT

Prior to the evaluation, informed consent was obtained. Ms. Mills was informed that she would not be asked to perform any activity she did not feel she could perform and she could stop any test secondary to pain, if she desired. She was instructed to exert her best effort on each test with no pain increase.

PROBLEM

Ms. Mills reports an injury on [DOB-3934] and she reports the following information about her injury: Primary Injury: Neck Side: Left

SHOULDER:

X Rotator Cuff, Impingement Syndrome, Labrum Repair - Arthroscope CERVICAL SPINE: X Surgical HNP - Stenosis - Spondylolysis - Spondylolisthesis - Instability

History: Ms. Mills reports that on 4-26-2019 she slipped and fell while entering a bank. She experienced immediate onset of left neck and shoulder pain. She was taken to the local Emergency room and was diagnosed with cervical sprain and left shoulder contusion. She underwent a cervical CT which was negative for fracture or dislocation.

On 6-21-19 she underwent a cervical MRI which demonstrated C3-4 bone spur, disc protrusion and mild to moderate foraminal stenosis.

She underwent an EMG/NCV which demonstrated chronic C5 radiculopathy.

On 1-21-2020 she underwent C3-4 discectomy and fusion.

Mills, Amy 3 Mills1364




She underwent left shoulder arthroscopic SLAP repair, performed by Dr. Khalily.

She continues to complain of constant neck and left shoulder pain which is aggravated with end range movement, lifting, pushing and pulling.

She was working as cosmetic instructor at the time of her injury. She states that her job required constant sitting, lifting up to 50 lbs, and frequent to constant reaching and hand manipulation.

She has been released by both her neck and shoulder surgeon. She was released with no formal work restrictions. She denies any previous neck or shoulder pathology.

GONIOMETRY

Mills, Amy 4

Mills1365




Goniometry measurements were taken according to protocol described in Guides to the Evaluation of Permanent Impairment, AMA, 5th Edition, 2001. The patient is asked to move their joint as far as they are able to move and their maximum bending angle is documented. Repeat trial consistency is one of the primary validity criteria to determine if the results are valid and can be used as a measure of the patient's medical impairment and flexibility. Test validity is also determined by observing the patient's joint range of motion while they are distracted by another task. The data should not be used if the range of motion improves by distraction compared to the measured motion. Right to left deficits can be calculated to determine the amount of loss of motion due to the injury or disease. Goniometry may be used to determine improvement in a treatment or rehabilitation program. Ms. Mills’ results are listed below.

_ SHOULDER LEFT RIGHT Flexion 130.0 160.0 Extension 40.0 50.0 Adduction 40.0 40.0 Abduction 130.0 160.0 Internal Rotation 50.0 80.0 External Rotation 70.0 90.0

GAIT PATTERN

Ms. Mills does not use a cane during ambulation. Her speed is average, there is no splinting, no holding , the movement patterns are symmetrical, and there is good correlation with the pain rating. She exhibits no limp and her behavior is appropriate.

REPETITIVE MOVEMENT TESTS

The Repetitive Movement Tests (RMT) evaluate the patient's ability to perform four common work activities, Repetitive Bending, Squatting, Kneeling, and Overhead Reaching. They are asked to perform one repetition and 10 repetitions at a controlled speed for all three movements and 10 fast repetitions for Bending and Overhead Reaching. The test results are analyzed for each test sequence before proceeding to the next test sequence. Any test is stopped if pain increases significantly or at the patient's request. Consistency of the movement patterns, willingness to move, pain, spasm, symmetry of motion, velocity of motion, and end range stretch are critically evaluated and an estimation is made regarding the patient's ability to move during an 8 hour work shift. Additional RMT tests are performed on the Hands, Wrists, Forearms or Neck for injuries to those areas. The results of Ms. Mills' evaluation are list below.

DISTRACTED MOVEMENT PATTERNS

The patient's movement patterns are observed directly during any Pre-FCE activities, which may include a Musculoskeletal Evaluation, a Gait Analysis, and the Repetitive Movement Tests. They are then observed by distraction throughout the FCE. If there are any improvements in the movement patterns by distraction, including increased range of motion, increased velocity, improved quality, decreased holding, decreased splinting, decreased limping, or any other change which signifies an improvement in the patient's movement ability, or if the patient's pain behavior decreases or their affect improves compared to the Pre-FCE activities, the Musculoskeletal Evaluation, the Gait Analysis or the Repetitive Movement Tests, it indicates that the patient's movement ability is better than what was demonstrated by direct observation when they were given ample opportunity to demonstrate their best performance. This represents a failed validity criteria, a non-organic sign, and suggests that the patient is attempting to control the test results to demonstrate more pain and disability than are actually present, the motivation of which is not known.

SUMMARY OF OBSERVED DISTRACTED MOVEMENT PATTERNS Ms. Mills' movement patterns did not change by distraction during the remainder of the FCE; therefore, the results

of the Repetitive Movement Tests are considered valid.

OCCASIONAL AND INFREQUENT MATERIAL HANDLING TESTS (OMH AND IMH The tests for Infrequent and Occasional Material Handling are documented in The Design of Manual Handlin Tasks: Revised Tables of Maximum Acceptable Weights and Forces, S.H. Snook and V.M. Ciriello, Ergonomics, 1991. IMH is defined as 1-4 lifts per day and OMH is defined as 5-32 lifts per day, spread out over the course of the day. The evaluator makes the decision regarding how many tests are requested from the patient, but a routine protocol involves all postures tested for OMH and only selected postures tested for IMH. Up to four decision making paradigms may be used for the IMH and OMH weights, Psychophysical, Kinesiophysical, Statistical and Computerized. Psychophysical is a form of testing where the patient chooses the acceptable weights based on their pain, their anxiety regarding reinjury, their level of strength and fitness and their inherent motivation to perform material handling following their injury. Kinesiophysical is a form of testing where the evaluator makes the decision about the acceptable weight considering items such as, the medical impairment, confirmed pain levels, range of motion, velocity of movement, body mechanics and forced body mechanic changes, the appearance of muscle strain, muscle recruitment patterns, counterbalancing strategies, patient stability and the general appearance

Mills, Amy 6 Mills1367




of overload. The Statistical approach considers databases and known statistical patterns which can show whether or not a person is working within acceptable patterns for their sex, strength levels and fitness levels. The fourth decision making method is Computerized Isoinertial Lifting (CIL). CIL objectively documents the acceleration and deceleration forces and patterns which provides the most accurate measures of pain inhibition and biomechanical overload.

The IMH and OMH tests are started at submaximal weights and the weight is progressively increased at safe levels until the proper weight is identified for each frequency. The patient is not asked to complete any test they feel is too difficult or which would place them at risk of injury, but they are asked to exert their best effort to achieve their maximum safe lifting ability.

The Maximum Weight Lifted (MWL) is the heaviest weight the patient completes or attempts for each posture. The OMH Result Weight is the weight that all of the data identifies as the safe and acceptable weight for each posture for handling 5-32 times per day and the IMH weight is the safe and acceptable weight for selected postures for 1-4 times per day. The Percentile Rank is how the patient compares to Snook and Ciriello's Database of Healthy, Industrial Norms. The OMH-HR is the heart rate documented for the OMH weight and the IMH/OMH Pain is the amount of pain reported by the patient for the IMH and OMH weights. Lifting and Carrying are recorded by weight and Pushing and Pulling are recorded by force exerted, not the weight or mass of the object moved. The worker's OMH weights are compared to the Dictionary of Occupational Titles, U.S. Department of Labor, 1991, Vol. I and I, called the DOT Physical Demand Levels, which are shown below.

DOT CLASSIFICATION OMH WEIGHTS _ Sedentary 1-10 Ib Light 11-20 Ib Medium 21-50 Ib Heavy 51-100 Ib Very Heavy over 100 Ib

Extrapolations are also made between the five DOT categories to offer a more specific description of the worker's ability. These new categories are shown below with the DOT Categories.

STRENGTH CLASSIFICATION OQMH WEIGHTS

_ Sedentary 1-10 Ib Sedentary-Light 11-15 Ib Light 16-20 Ib Light-Medium 21-35 |b Medium 36-50 Ib Medium-Heavy 51-75 Ib Heavy 76-100 Ib Very Heavy over 100 Ib

HAND TESTS

A series of tests are performed for Maximum Grip And Pinch Strength described in Grip and Pinch Strength Normative Data for Adults, V. Mathiowetz, et.al., Arch PM&R, 1985, and tests for Gross and Fine Motor Control using the Purdue Pegboard. There are a number of validity criteria indicating the degree of effort documented in The Seriously Uninjured Hand: Weakness of Grip, H. M. Stokes, JOM, 1983, Detecting Sincerity of Effort When Measuring Grip Strength, B. Niebuhr and R. Marion, Am. J. PM, 1987, Detection of Submaximal Effort by Use of the Rapid Exchange Grip, D. Hildreth, et.al., J. Hand Surg., 1989, and Functional Capaci Evaluation: The Procedure Manual, Keith L. Blankenship, Macon, GA, 1994. The Hand Grip and Pinch Grip tests may also be repeated a second or third time in order to verify the validity of the patient's effort and to evaluate for pain and fatigue responses.

HAND FUNCTION CLASSIFICATION

The patient's ability to use their Upper Extremities and Hands for work is calculated by an algorithm including their demonstrated strength on the Static Strength Tests and the Occasional Material Handling Tests, their Fine Motor Skills scored by the Purdue Pegboard and their observed skill throughout the FCE. The patient's Hand Function is classified on the table below.

HAND FUNCTION CLASSIFICATION LEFT RIGHT Simple Grasp XX XX Fine Motor Skills XX XX Low Speed Assembly XX XX PURDUE PEGBOARD ASSEMBLY TEST TRIAL | TRIAL 2 TRIAL 3 AVERAGE PERCENTILE 29 29.0 5%

The results of the Purdue Pegboard Assembly Test indicate that Ms. Mills has good fine motor skills and is qualified for Assembly Tasks of pieces in the 1-4 mm. range or larger at a Non Production Rate; therefore, she is classified at the Low Speed Assembly classification.

NON-MATERIAL HANDLING TESTS (NMH The patient is tested on the selected work stations for the Non-Material Handling and Repetitive Activities. In many cases, the data can be extrapolated from the Material Handling Tests and other tests such as the Repetitive Movement tests. The amount of time spent Standing, Walking, and Sitting is obtained from the Activity Profile and the amount of time the patient actually performs these activities throughout the FCE. Stair Climbing, Balancing, and Crawling are directly tested. The ability to use Arm or Leg controls is tested directly or the data is extrapolated from the Static Strength Tests and/or the Material Handling Tests according to the criteria in the Dictionary of Occupational Titles, U.S. Department of Labor, 1991. Professional judgment is required for the NMH decisions since time does not permit exhaustive testing for each activity and there is no published research or database to assist with these decisions. There are also an extremely limited number of validity criteria for the NMH activities. This is a more subjective part of the FCE process; therefore, patient cooperation is required for reliable results. Ms. Mills' results are listed on the table below.

BODY MECHANICS

The patient's utilization of body mechanics is observed throughout the evaluation. The body mechanics they use intuitively when distracted usually represents what they use naturally during work and leisure activities, even though they may report using a different style. Based on their injury and the amount of weight they are capable of lifting, recommendations may be made in order to decrease the risk for future injury relative to improper body mechanics. PERCENTILE OF STRENGTH PROFILE

The percentiles of strength for all of the activities tested are analyzed for specific variations. Most individuals will have a percentile ranking which is reasonably consistent across all tests. Specific variations may indicate weakness secondary to pain, neurological deficits, disuse atrophy, poor cardiovascular fitness, or specific lifestyle trends. The patient's strength may be characterized by the word descriptors on the table below.

The patient's heart rate is documented at periodic intervals throughout testing. The data is used to determine the patient's suitability for work relative to their level of cardiovascular fitness, which is measured while the patient is actually working. This profile is felt to be more appropriate than a low level fitness evaluation performed on steps, treadmill, or bicycle ergometer because this profile is specific to work activities. Ifthe patient's heart rates exceed their acceptable levels, a cardiovascular fitness program may be necessary prior to return to work. All Continuous Work categories assume that normal breaks are permitted. The acceptable heart rate limits for continuous work are shown below.

CONTINUOUS WORKING TIME PERCENT MAX H.R.

_ Continuous Sitting 30-45% Continuous Standing 35-50% Continuous Walking 45-55% Material Handling 10 min. 75-85% Material Handling 30 min. 67-75% Material Handling 60 min. 60-66% Material Handling 4 hrs. 57-63% Material Handling 8 hrs. 55-60%

THE BLANKENSHIP RELIABILITY PROFILE

The Blankenship Reliability Profile includes two objective components, Non Organic Signs and Validity. Patients scoring invalid on both components are felt to be attempting to control the test results to demonstrate a greater level of disability than what is actually present, the motivation of which is not known. These may not be reported if insufficient data exists.

SYMPTOM /DISABILITY EXAGGERATION PROFILE

Symptom/Disability Exaggeration is a clinical behavior in which the patient's symptoms, pain behavior and disability are out of proportion to their medical impairment and movement patterns observed mainly by distraction. This assessment begins with the patient completing questionnaires which help the evaluator understand the patient's perspective of their symptoms and disability. Next, an intake interview is conducted and the patient's description of their symptoms and disability are correlated with their actual medical impairment. Then, the FCE is conducted and the following observations of the patient are made.

True Pain Behavior

Overreaction Behavior

The Movement Patterns of the Injured Body Part

The Patient's Symptomatic Reports

Non Organic Test Results

True Strength and Strength Deficits

Movement Patterns that Improve by Distraction

General Attitude

Motivation to Cooperate

0. Deliberate Use of Poor Body Mechanics that Increase Stress on the Injured Body Part

BeerNavawn-

Any significant lack of correlation between the patient's perception of their symptoms and disability and the actual test results, including the observation of normal to near normal movement patterns observed either by direct observation or by distraction, represents Symptom/Disability Exaggeration Behavior. Observing the movement patterns of the injured body part is the best way to determine if any significant level of pain and disability exists due to the neurophysiological relationship that exists between pain and the ability to move. When significant pain is present, the patient is unable to move normally, and when there is no pain, normal movement patterns are possible. Therefore, if a patient reports a high level of pain and disability, but the movement patterns of their injured body part are normal, this is neurophysiologically impossible and the patient is demonstrates Symptom/Disability Exaggerator behavior. The motivation for this behavior is unknown and cannot be discovered by the FCE alone, but one of the most blatant acts of Symptom/Disability Exaggeration behavior is abnormal movement patterns and pain affect demonstrated by direct observation which improve significantly by distraction. The patient must offer a suitable explanation for this finding, or conscious malingering may be assumed.

Since professional judgment is involved, Symptom/Disability Exaggeration behavior is not documented unless there

Mills, Amy 12 Mills1373




is a significant disparity between the patient's subjective reports and behavior and the evaluator's observations of the patient's actual movement patterns, general behavior and test results. Symptom/Disability Exaggeration behavior generally represents an obstacle to effective rehabilitation and return to work, and an effort should be placed on determining why this inappropriate clinical behavior exists.

The Validity Profile is comprised of a cohort of individual tests that collectively help determine whether or not the patient is exerting their best effort during all of the FCE tests. Effort is defined as the physical ability and motivation to complete a task within the individual's pain tolerance. A significant increase in pain is not required. If the patient exerts effort up to the point of a barely perceptible pain increase, or slightly below that level so there is no pain increase at all, then they will pass the overall Validity Profile. If the patient does not pass the overall Validity Profile, then they have not exerted their best effort. The patient cannot assert that they were not able to exert their best effort due to pain since they are not asked to tolerate any pain increase at all, or at least no more than a barely perceptible increase, which everyone can perceive. And since the patient is not asked to perform tasks for which they do not have the physical ability, or if they do not have the physical ability, the test data should reveal

Mills, Amy 13 Mills1374




that, then the only reason for not passing the overall Validity Profile is that the patient was not motivated to cooperate with the evaluation process and exert their best effort. The patient must then explain why they were not motivated to cooperate with the testing process to demonstrate their current ability to work, and their reason may not be secondary to pain. Therefore, failing the Overall Validity Profile is viewed as a voluntary act of non-compliance with the testing process and with the professionals who requested the test. Current research, submitted to Spine, shows that a strong indicator of whether or not an evaluee is cooperating with and exerting their best effort on a functional test is the Evaluator's professional judgment; however, most of the Validity Criteria of The Blankenship System FCE are based on published research. A few are based on K. Blankenship's unpublished research. The Blankenship System FCE Bibliography sites the published and unpublished works.

It is believed that a reliable overall Validity Profile should contain at least 20 validity criteria. A Validity Profile of less than 20 may also be reliable, but the evaluator must add their professional judgment regarding the reliability of the test results.

There is also an empirical relationship between the number of validity criteria passed and the degree of effort

exerted during testing and the reliability of the test results for predicting work performance. This algorithm was developed by K. Blankenship published in the revised edition of, The Blankenship System Functional Capaci Evaluation: The Protocol Manual, revision began in 1996, Macon, GA. That revised algorithm is shown below.

Validity Criteria Passed Degree of Effort Word Descriptor

90-100% Excellent Effort Valid Results

80-89% Good Effort Valid Results

70-79% Fair Effort Valid Results

70-75% Borderline Valid, Results are Conservative

60-69% Poor Effort Borderline Invalid Results

<60% Very Poor Effort Invalid Results < 20 Criteria May Be Unreliable, Professional Judgment Required

medical bills and health insurance, Medicaid, or Medicare records, concerning any medical treatment that I have received from you, at your institution, as well as all such records which you keep in the regular course of business that are found in my medical records file, hereby authorize the release of all records regarding mental health, psychiatric (other than psychotherapy notes which must be requested by separate authorization), chemical dependency or HIV. A photostatic copy hereof shall be as valid as the original. I hereby authorize a free copy of my medical records, pursuant to KRS 422,317, be sent to the extent that I have not already requested my one fiee copy.

The purpose of this authorization and request is to permit MORRIS & PLAYER pitc or any of its representatives to obtain ALL medical information pertaining to my physical or mental condition, The authorization expires three (3) years from the date of the signature. The aforementioned expiration date has not passed, as this matter is ongoing,

Thereby authorize MORRIS & PLAYER ric or any of its representatives to speak to my healthcare professionals privately or to take testimony at deposition or trial as may be requested,

[ hereby authorize MORRIS & PLAYER putc or any of its representatives to inspect all documents in the possession of the above-listed healthcare provider that are in any way related to me or my health care,

I have the right to revoke this authorization in writing by providing a signed, written notice of revocation to the health care provider listed above and to MORRIS & PLAYER pute. However, I understand that if { revoke this authorization, it will have no effect on the actions taken by the medical provider in reliance on it before il he or she receives notice of my revocation. Medical providers may not condition treatment or payment on whether the above- listed patient executes this authorization. The information disclosed pursuant to this authorization may be subjected to re-disclosure and no longer protected by the privacy regulations promulgated pursuant to the Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA).
